The regression model for the frequency of doctor visits, health examinations, and whether the same physician is sought for medical care has demonstrated significant correlations with gender, age and education-level variables. Four health behaviors (i.e., exercise habits, dietary habits, regular blood pressure measurement, drinking habits) exhibited a significant correlation with healthcare utilization (P<0.05).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted intervention promoting regular exercise habits and healthy dietary habits among individuals with low education levels will result in a significant reduction in the frequency of outpatient visits over a 12-month period, compared to a control group without such an intervention.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found significant correlations between certain health behaviors (exercise habits, dietary habits, regular blood pressure measurement, drinking habits) and healthcare utilization, as well as with demographic variables such as gender, age, and education level. The analysis revealed that these factors influence the frequency of doctor visits, health examinations, and whether patients seek medical care from the same physician.

GAP: While the study established correlations between health behaviors and healthcare utilization, it does not explore the potential causal relationships or the mechanisms by which specific health behaviors influence the use of medical resources. Additionally, the study does not examine how interventions targeting these health behaviors might impact healthcare costs or outcomes.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that a targeted intervention promoting regular exercise habits and healthy dietary habits among individuals with low education levels will result in a significant reduction in the frequency of outpatient visits over a 12-month period, compared to a control group without such an intervention.

abstract: The purpose of this study is to determine if baseline antral follicle assessment may serve as additional information in predicting in vitro fertilization outcome. Prospective, descriptive preliminary study of in vitro fertilization outcome. From July 1998 to July 1999, 224 patients underwent antral follicle assessment (follicle 2-6 mm in diameter) on baseline of the planned, stimulated in vitro fertilization cycle. The outcomes were analyzed with respect to antral follicle assessment (<or = 6 or>6), basal cycle day 3 follicle stimulated hormone (<or = 10 or>10 IU/L) and maternal age (<or = 35 or>35 years). The clinical pregnancy rate was significantly higher in the group with baseline antral follicle>6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle<or = 6 (51% vs. 19%, respectively). Controlling for patient age, and basal follicle stimulated hormone, the pregnancy rate was significantly higher in the group with antral follicle>6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle<or = 6. The cancellation rate was significantly increased with advancing maternal age, elevated basal follicle stimulated hormone levels, and baseline antral follicle<or = 6. The cancellation rate was significantly higher in the group with antral follicle<or = 6 compared to that in the group with antral follicle>or = 6 (33% vs. 1%, respectively).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that supplementation with specific nutrients or hormones that promote antral follicle growth will increase the baseline antral follicle count and subsequently improve clinical pregnancy rates in women undergoing in vitro fertilization, particularly in those with initially low antral follicle counts.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that patients with a baseline antral follicle count greater than 6 have a significantly higher clinical pregnancy rate (51%) compared to those with a count of 6 or less (19%), and that this association holds even when controlling for patient age and basal follicle-stimulating hormone levels. Additionally, the cancellation rate was higher in patients with an antral follicle count of 6 or less, advancing maternal age, and elevated basal follicle-stimulating hormone levels.

GAP: While the study establishes a correlation between antral follicle count and IVF outcomes, it does not explore the potential biological mechanisms underlying this relationship, such as how antral follicle count affects oocyte quality or embryo development. Furthermore, the study does not investigate whether interventions aimed at improving antral follicle count could enhance IVF success rates.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that supplementation with specific nutrients or hormones that promote antral follicle growth will increase the baseline antral follicle count and subsequently improve clinical pregnancy rates in women undergoing in vitro fertilization, particularly in those with initially low antral follicle counts.

abstract: First, to establish whether a deprivation gradient in all-cause mortality exists for all ethnic groups within New Zealand; second, if such gradients do exist, whether their absolute slopes are the same; and third, if such gradients exist, what impact the unequal deprivation distributions of the different ethnic groups have on the observed ethnic inequalities in life expectancy at birth. Abridged lifetables for the period 1999-2003 were constructed using standard demographic methods for each of four ethnic groups (Asian, Pacific, Maori and European) by NZDep2001 quintile and sex. Gradients were estimated by fitting generalised linear models to the quintile-specific life expectancy estimates for each ethnic group (by sex). The contribution of variation in deprivation distributions to inter-ethnic inequalities in life expectancy was estimated by re-weighting the quintile-specific mortality rates for each ethnic group using weights derived from the European deprivation distribution and recalculating the lifetable. All four ethnic groups exhibit deprivation gradients in all-cause mortality (life expectancy). Maori show the steepest gradients, with slopes approximately 25% steeper than those of Europeans for both males and females. By contrast, gradients among Asian and Pacific peoples are shallower than those of their European counterparts.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the differential access to healthcare services among ethnic groups, as measured by the proportion of individuals with health insurance and regular healthcare check-ups, will mediate the relationship between socioeconomic deprivation and life expectancy, resulting in steeper deprivation gradients for Maori and shallower gradients for Asian and Pacific peoples compared to Europeans.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that all four ethnic groups in New Zealand (Asian, Pacific, Maori, and European) exhibit deprivation gradients in all-cause mortality, with Maori showing the steepest gradients and Asian and Pacific peoples showing shallower gradients compared to Europeans. The slopes of these gradients vary among ethnic groups, with Maori having slopes approximately 25% steeper than those of Europeans for both males and females.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of what specific factors contribute to the observed differences in the slopes of deprivation gradients among ethnic groups. While the study demonstrates that deprivation distributions affect life expectancy, it does not explore the underlying causes of these differences, such as potential variations in access to healthcare, socioeconomic status, or environmental factors.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the differential access to healthcare services among ethnic groups, as measured by the proportion of individuals with health insurance and regular healthcare check-ups, will mediate the relationship between socioeconomic deprivation and life expectancy, resulting in steeper deprivation gradients for Maori and shallower gradients for Asian and Pacific peoples compared to Europeans.

abstract: Unicompartmental replacement can be an alternative to tibial osteotomy in younger, active patients with unicompartmental knee disease. In unicompartmental replacement, the other compartments and knee ligaments are largely untouched. Therefore, it was hypothesized that the knee kinematics after unicompartmental replacement may also be unchanged. To test this hypothesis, knee kinematics and quadriceps tension were recorded before and after replacement with a unicompartmental design and then with a tricompartmental design. Six human cadaver knees were tested before implantation, after implantation with a bicruciate-retaining unicompartmental knee prosthesis, and after implantation with a posterior cruciate-retaining tricompartmental knee prosthesis. The unicompartmental prosthesis was initially implanted, and it was then revised to a total condylar knee replacement. The knee kinematics were measured with use of an electromagnetic tracking device while the knee was put through dynamic simulated stair-climbing under peak flexion moments of approximately 40 N-m. Quadriceps tension was also measured for all three conditions. No significant differences in tibial axial rotation were noted between the intact and unicompartmental conditions. However, tricompartmental replacement significantly affected tibial axial rotation (p = 0.001). Femoral rollback was not significantly affected by either unicompartmental or tricompartmental arthroplasty. Quadriceps tension was also similar among all three conditions.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients undergoing unicompartmental knee replacement will exhibit a lower rate of degenerative changes in the unaffected knee compartments at a 5-year follow-up compared to those undergoing tricompartmental replacement, as measured by MRI and clinical assessment of osteoarthritis progression.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that unicompartmental knee replacement does not significantly affect tibial axial rotation or quadriceps tension compared to the intact knee, whereas tricompartmental replacement significantly alters tibial axial rotation. Additionally, neither unicompartmental nor tricompartmental arthroplasty significantly affects femoral rollback during dynamic simulated stair-climbing.

GAP: This study leaves unanswered how these findings translate to in vivo conditions, particularly in terms of long-term functional outcomes and patient satisfaction. It also does not explore the impact of these different surgical approaches on the progression of degenerative changes in the unaffected compartments of the knee.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients undergoing unicompartmental knee replacement will exhibit a lower rate of degenerative changes in the unaffected knee compartments at a 5-year follow-up compared to those undergoing tricompartmental replacement, as measured by MRI and clinical assessment of osteoarthritis progression.

abstract: There are 71 previously untreated patients with cytological or histological evidence of primary lung cancer who were admitted to the oncology department between November 2013 and August 2014. Forty-five healthy individuals with age, sex and BMI matching the lung cancer patients, were recruited to take part in the study as a control group. Leptin levels were measured quantitatively by using a microELISA kit. The serum leptin levels at diagnosis were significantly lower in lung cancer patients than those in control subjects (4.75±4.91 ng/ml, 9.67±8.02 ng/ml; p<0.001). We did not find any significant difference in leptin values related to clinicopathological parameters such as ECOG PS, weight loss, histological type, disease stage and TNM classification. Nevertheless, we demonstrated a significant correlation between serum leptin levels and BMI in lung cancer patients (correlation coefficient: 0.303; p>0.010). The analysis of serum leptin values did not show any association with the overall survival of the patients.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic changes in serum leptin levels during the first six months of treatment will predict treatment response and prognosis in lung cancer patients, with increasing leptin levels correlating with better treatment outcomes.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract include significantly lower serum leptin levels in lung cancer patients compared to healthy controls, a significant correlation between serum leptin levels and BMI in lung cancer patients, and no association between serum leptin levels and overall survival or clinicopathological parameters such as ECOG PS, weight loss, histological type, disease stage, and TNM classification.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of how serum leptin levels change over time in lung cancer patients, particularly in response to treatment, and whether these changes have any prognostic value. Additionally, the underlying mechanisms behind the observed correlation between serum leptin levels and BMI in lung cancer patients are not explored.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that dynamic changes in serum leptin levels during the first six months of treatment will predict treatment response and prognosis in lung cancer patients, with increasing leptin levels correlating with better treatment outcomes.

abstract: To investigate if fetal gender (1) affects the risk of having an emergency department (ED) visit for asthma; and (2) is associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes among women who had at least one visit to the ED for asthma during pregnancy. We linked two provincial administrative databases containing records on in-patient deliveries and ED visits. The study sample included women who delivered a live singleton baby between April 2003 and March 2004. Pregnant women who made at least one ED visit for asthma were counted as cases and the rest of the women as control subjects. We performed a multivariable analysis using logistic regression to model the risk of having an ED visit for asthma, with fetal gender being one of the predictors. In addition, a series of multivariable logistic regressions were also constructed separately for cases and controls for the following adverse delivery outcomes: low birth weight baby, preterm delivery, and delivery via Caesarian section. Among 109,173 live singleton deliveries, 530 women had visited ED due to asthma during pregnancy. While having an ED visit for asthma was positively associated with teenage pregnancy, low income, and presence of pregnancy-induced hypertension, it was not associated with fetal gender (OR 1.01, 95% CI 0.85-1.19). Fetal gender was not a significant predictor of adverse pregnancy outcomes among women who had an asthma ED visit during pregnancy.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the severity of asthma, as measured by the number of ED visits for asthma during pregnancy, will modify the relationship between fetal gender and the risk of low birth weight, such that male fetuses will be at increased risk of low birth weight compared to female fetuses when mothers experience multiple ED visits for asthma during pregnancy.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that having an emergency department (ED) visit for asthma during pregnancy is associated with factors such as teenage pregnancy, low income, and pregnancy-induced hypertension, but not with fetal gender. Additionally, fetal gender was not a significant predictor of adverse pregnancy outcomes (low birth weight baby, preterm delivery, and delivery via Caesarian section) among women who had an asthma ED visit during pregnancy.

GAP: The study does not investigate the potential impact of asthma severity or the timing of ED visits for asthma during pregnancy on the relationship between fetal gender and pregnancy outcomes. This leaves unanswered questions about whether specific characteristics of asthma episodes could modify the association between fetal gender and adverse pregnancy outcomes.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the severity of asthma, as measured by the number of ED visits for asthma during pregnancy, will modify the relationship between fetal gender and the risk of low birth weight, such that male fetuses will be at increased risk of low birth weight compared to female fetuses when mothers experience multiple ED visits for asthma during pregnancy.

abstract: This study sought to evaluate mutations in genes encoding the slow component of the cardiac delayed rectifier K+ current (I(Ks)) channel in familial atrial fibrillation (AF). Although AF can have a genetic etiology, links between inherited gene defects and acquired factors such as atrial stretch have not been explored. Mutation screening of the KCNQ1, KCNE1, KCNE2, and KCNE3 genes was performed in 50 families with AF. The effects of mutant protein on cardiac I(Ks) activation were evaluated using electrophysiological studies and human atrial action potential modeling. One missense KCNQ1 mutation, R14C, was identified in 1 family with a high prevalence of hypertension. Atrial fibrillation was present only in older individuals who had developed atrial dilation and who were genotype positive. Patch-clamp studies of wild-type or R14C KCNQ1 expressed with KCNE1 in CHO cells showed no statistically significant differences between wild-type and mutant channel kinetics at baseline, or after activation of adenylate cyclase with forskolin. After exposure to hypotonic solution to elicit cell swelling/stretch, mutant channels showed a marked increase in current, a leftward shift in the voltage dependence of activation, altered channel kinetics, and shortening of the modeled atrial action potential duration.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the R14C KCNQ1 mutation will exhibit enhanced sensitivity to mechanical stress induced by atrial stretch, resulting in a significant increase in the incidence of atrial fibrillation in genotype-positive individuals with comorbid hypertension, compared to those without hypertension, and that this effect will be mediated by altered regulation of the KCNQ1-KCNE1 complex by stretch-activated signaling pathways.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study include the identification of a missense KCNQ1 mutation, R14C, in a family with a high prevalence of hypertension, where atrial fibrillation was present only in older individuals with atrial dilation who were genotype positive. Additionally, patch-clamp studies showed that mutant channels exhibited altered kinetics and increased current after exposure to hypotonic solution, but not at baseline or after activation of adenylate cyclase with forskolin.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of how the R14C KCNQ1 mutation interacts with other acquired factors, such as hypertension and atrial stretch, to contribute to the development of atrial fibrillation, and what specific molecular mechanisms underlie the observed changes in channel kinetics and current in response to cell swelling.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the R14C KCNQ1 mutation will exhibit enhanced sensitivity to mechanical stress induced by atrial stretch, resulting in a significant increase in the incidence of atrial fibrillation in genotype-positive individuals with comorbid hypertension, compared to those without hypertension, and that this effect will be mediated by altered regulation of the KCNQ1-KCNE1 complex by stretch-activated signaling pathways.

abstract: Weight scales as height squared, which is an observation that forms the basis of body mass index (weight/height(2)). If, and how, circumferences, including waist circumference (WC) and hip circumference (HC), scale to height remains unclear, but this is an important consideration when developing normative ranges or applying WC/height and HC/height as risk indexes. The study aim was to examine the scaling of weight, WC, and HC to height in NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey) III participants. Subjects were adult non-Hispanic white, non-Hispanic black, and Mexican American men (n = 7422) and nonpregnant women (n = 7999) who had complete demographic and anthropometric data. In addition to height, allometric models were developed for each measure that controlled for age, race, and self-reported health status. After adjustment for age and race, weight scaled to height in men and women with mean (±SEE) powers of 2.29 ± 0.11 and 1.80 ± 0.07, respectively (both P<0.001). Although univariate circumference-height models were weak or nonsignificant, when adjusted for age and race WC and HC scaled to height with powers of 0.76 ± 0.08 and 0.45 ± 0.05, respectively, in men and 0.80 ± 0.05 and 0.53 ± 0.04, respectively, in women (all P<0.001). Age- and race-adjusted incremental increases in circumferences ranged from 0.2 to 0.5 cm per centimeter increase in height. Both WC/height and HC/height scaled negatively to height in men and women, and WC/HC scaled negatively to height in women only (all P<0.001). Health status-adjusted models were similar.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling powers of weight, waist circumference, and hip circumference to height will change significantly during puberty, with the powers increasing in early adolescence and stabilizing in late adolescence to reach the adult values observed in this study.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that weight scales to height with different powers for men and women, and that waist circumference (WC) and hip circumference (HC) scale to height with lower powers, also differing between men and women. The study also found that WC/height and HC/height scale negatively to height, and that WC/HC scales negatively to height in women. The powers of scaling were determined to be: weight ~ height^2.29 for men and ~ height^1.80 for women, WC ~ height^0.76 for men and ~ height^0.80 for women, and HC ~ height^0.45 for men and ~ height^0.53 for women.

GAP: While the study established the scaling relationships between height and various body measurements, it did not investigate how these scaling relationships might change or develop across different stages of life, particularly during childhood and adolescence when growth patterns can significantly influence adult body proportions.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the scaling powers of weight, waist circumference, and hip circumference to height will change significantly during puberty, with the powers increasing in early adolescence and stabilizing in late adolescence to reach the adult values observed in this study.

abstract: We explored whether QT corrected dispersion (QTcD) can identify left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) in hypertensives. We enrolled 100 hypertensive patients (study group) and 30 normotensive subjects (control group). Echocardiography was performed to measure left ventricular mass and left ventricular mass index. Electrocardiogram was performed to measure QTcD. LVH was present in 42 patients (42%) of the study group, none among controls. Hypertensive patients had significantly greater indices of LVH and QTcD compared with controls (p<0.001 for all). Similarly, among hypertensive patients, those with LVH had a significantly greater QTcD compared with those without (p<0.001). Pearson's correlation coefficient test demonstrated strongly positive correlations between QTcD and the indices of LVH (p<0.001 for all). Analysis of the receiver operating characteristic curves identified 60 ms as the optimal cut-off value of QTcD that best predicts LVH in hypertensives. Using this value, QTcD was able to predict LVH with a sensitivity of 92.9% and specificity 98.2%.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in hypertensive patients with left ventricular hypertrophy, a reduction in QTcD of at least 10 ms after 6 months of antihypertensive treatment will be associated with a significant decrease in left ventricular mass index, as measured by echocardiography.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that QT corrected dispersion (QTcD) is significantly greater in hypertensive patients than in normotensive subjects, and among hypertensive patients, those with left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) have a greater QTcD than those without. Additionally, QTcD is strongly positively correlated with indices of LVH and can predict LVH with high sensitivity and specificity when using a cut-off value of 60 ms.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of whether QTcD can be used to monitor the progression of LVH over time in hypertensive patients, or whether changes in QTcD correlate with changes in left ventricular mass index in response to antihypertensive therapy.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that in hypertensive patients with left ventricular hypertrophy, a reduction in QTcD of at least 10 ms after 6 months of antihypertensive treatment will be associated with a significant decrease in left ventricular mass index, as measured by echocardiography.

abstract: Traumatic aortic injury (TAI) is a rare but life-threatening type of injury. We investigate whether the anatomy of the aortic arch influences the severity of aortic injury. This is a retrospective study of twenty-two cases treated with TEVAR for TAI in our department from 2009 to 2014. Aortic injury was assessed in accordance with the recommendations of the Society of Vascular Surgery. We measured the aortic arch angle and the aortic arch index, based on the initial angio-CT scan, in each of the analyzed cases. The mean aortic arch index and mean aortic arch angle were 6.8 cm and 58.3°, respectively, in the type I injury group; 4.4 cm and 45.9° in the type III group; 3.3 cm and 37° in the type IV group. There were substantial differences in both the aortic arch index and the aortic arch angle of the type III and IV groups. A multivariate analysis confirmed that the aortic arch angle was significantly associated with the occurrence of type III damage (OR 1.5; 95% CI 1.03-2.2).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that a more acute aortic arch angle will result in a higher concentration of mechanical stress on the inner curvature of the aortic arch during simulated traumatic events, as measured by finite element analysis, thereby increasing the likelihood of type III TAI.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found significant differences in the aortic arch index and angle among different types of traumatic aortic injuries (TAI), with the aortic arch angle being significantly associated with the occurrence of type III damage. Specifically, the mean aortic arch angle was 58.3° for type I injuries, 45.9° for type III injuries, and 37° for type IV injuries. Multivariate analysis confirmed the association between the aortic arch angle and type III damage, with an odds ratio of 1.5.

GAP: While the study establishes a correlation between the aortic arch angle and the severity of TAI, it does not investigate the underlying biomechanical mechanisms by which the aortic arch anatomy influences the type and severity of aortic injuries. Specifically, it remains unclear how variations in aortic arch geometry affect the distribution of mechanical stresses on the aortic wall during traumatic events.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that a more acute aortic arch angle will result in a higher concentration of mechanical stress on the inner curvature of the aortic arch during simulated traumatic events, as measured by finite element analysis, thereby increasing the likelihood of type III TAI.

abstract: This study aimed to evaluate the utility of risk stratification of gynecologic oncology patients with neutropenic fever (NF). A retrospective chart review of gynecologic cancer patients admitted with NF from 2007 to 2011 was performed, wherein demographic, oncologic, and NF characteristics (hospitalization length, complications, and death) were collected. The Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer (MASCC) risk index score was calculated; low risk was considered ≥ 21. SAS 9.2 was used for statistical analyses. Eighty-three patients met the study criteria. Most (92%) were Caucasian and had advanced stage disease (71%). Primary tumors were 58% ovary, 35% endometrium, and 6% cervix. All patients were receiving chemotherapy on admission (72% for primary, 28% for recurrent disease). Forty-eight percent had a positive culture, and most (58%) positive cultures were urine. Seventy-six percent of patients were considered low risk. High-risk patients were more likely to have a severe complication (10% versus 50%, p=0.0003), multiple severe complications (3% versus 20%, p=0.0278), ICU admission (2% versus 40%, p<0.0001), overall mortality (2% versus 15%, p=0.0417), and death due to neutropenic fever (0% versus 15%, p=0.0124). MASCC had a positive predictive value of 50% and negative predictive value of 90%. The median MASCC score for all patients was 22 (range, 11-26), but the median MASCC score for those with death or a severe complication was 17 (range, 11-24).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that gynecologic oncology patients with neutropenic fever and a MASCC score ≤ 19 will have a significant reduction in severe complications and mortality when treated with an intensified, protocol-driven management strategy compared to standard care.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that the Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer (MASCC) risk index score is effective in stratifying gynecologic oncology patients with neutropenic fever (NF) into low and high-risk categories, with high-risk patients being more likely to experience severe complications, ICU admission, and mortality. The MASCC score had a positive predictive value of 50% and a negative predictive value of 90%. Patients with lower MASCC scores (median of 17) were more likely to experience death or severe complications.

GAP: The study does not investigate the specific factors that contribute to the difference in outcomes between patients with similar MASCC scores, particularly among those with advanced stage disease. It also does not explore whether interventions tailored to the MASCC risk category can improve outcomes in high-risk patients.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that gynecologic oncology patients with neutropenic fever and a MASCC score ≤ 19 will have a significant reduction in severe complications and mortality when treated with an intensified, protocol-driven management strategy compared to standard care.

abstract: This study aims to evaluate local failure patterns in node negative breast cancer patients treated with post-mastectomy radiotherapy including internal mammary chain only. Retrospective analysis of 92 internal or central-breast node-negative tumours with mastectomy and external irradiation of the internal mammary chain at the dose of 50 Gy, from 1994 to 1998. Local recurrence rate was 5 % (five cases). Recurrence sites were the operative scare and chest wall. Factors associated with increased risk of local failure were age<or = 40 years and tumour size greater than 20mm, without statistical significance.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the radiation dose to 60 Gy in node-negative breast cancer patients with tumors larger than 20mm and who are aged 40 or younger will result in a statistically significant reduction in local recurrence rates compared to the standard 50 Gy dose.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that in node-negative breast cancer patients treated with post-mastectomy radiotherapy including internal mammary chain, the local recurrence rate was 5%, with recurrences primarily at the operative scar and chest wall. Factors such as age ≤ 40 years and tumor size > 20mm were associated with an increased risk of local failure, although these associations were not statistically significant.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of whether the dose of 50 Gy is optimal for preventing local recurrence in this patient population, especially considering the factors associated with increased risk. It also does not explore the potential impact of individual variations in patient biology or tumor characteristics on the efficacy of the radiation therapy.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that increasing the radiation dose to 60 Gy in node-negative breast cancer patients with tumors larger than 20mm and who are aged 40 or younger will result in a statistically significant reduction in local recurrence rates compared to the standard 50 Gy dose.

abstract: Cyclical pamidronate therapy in a 2-year-old child with skeletal fragility resulted in remodelling of vertebral fractures and improvement in bone mineral density (BMD) at distal radial and spinal sites. The BMD at both sites decreased precipitously within 24 months of stopping treatment, raising the question as to whether bisphosphonates can be stopped in a growing child with skeletal fragility. At age 23 months, a male toddler sustained a low trauma fracture of his right femur. Skeletal radiographs revealed generalised osteopenia with multiple vertebral body fractures. He was diagnosed with type IV osteogenesis imperfecta; however, no mutations were found in COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes. This case report presents bone densitometry data before, during and after bisphosphonate treatment. Axial QCT was main outcome from 2 years of age; DXA and pQCT were taken after age 5. QCT confirmed that he had low spinal trabecular volumetric BMD (Z-score -2.4). After 4 years of treatment his vertebral fractures had been remodelled and all bone densitometry values (QCT, DXA and pQCT) were within normal range and therefore treatment was discontinued. Shortly after this he suffered stress fractures of his left mid tibia and at the sclerotic metaphyseal line corresponding to his first APD treatment. He had marked reduction in spinal trabecular and distal radial vBMD; change in BMAD was less marked.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuous, low-dose bisphosphonate therapy, rather than cyclical treatment, will maintain improved bone mineral density and prevent recurrent fractures in children with type IV osteogenesis imperfecta for at least 5 years after initiation of treatment, thereby reducing the risk of stress fractures and improving long-term skeletal health.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract include the effectiveness of cyclical pamidronate therapy in improving bone mineral density (BMD) and remodelling vertebral fractures in a child with skeletal fragility due to type IV osteogenesis imperfecta. However, upon discontinuation of the treatment, the child experienced a significant decrease in BMD and suffered new fractures, indicating a potential need for long-term or alternative treatment strategies.

GAP: A key knowledge gap left unanswered by this work is the optimal duration and potential long-term effects of bisphosphonate therapy in children with skeletal fragility, particularly in terms of the sustainability of bone health improvements once treatment is stopped. Additionally, the abstract raises questions about the underlying mechanisms of bone loss after treatment cessation and how to prevent or mitigate this effect.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that continuous, low-dose bisphosphonate therapy, rather than cyclical treatment, will maintain improved bone mineral density and prevent recurrent fractures in children with type IV osteogenesis imperfecta for at least 5 years after initiation of treatment, thereby reducing the risk of stress fractures and improving long-term skeletal health.

abstract: Hip fracture in geriatric patients has a substantial economic impact and represents a major cause of morbidity and mortality in this population. At our institution, a regional anesthesia program was instituted for patients undergoing surgery for hip fracture. This retrospective cohort review examines the effects of regional anesthesia (from mainly after July 2007) vs general anesthesia (mainly prior to July 2007) on morbidity, mortality and hospitalization costs. This retrospective cohort study involved data collection from electronic and paper charts of 308 patients who underwent surgery for hip fracture from September 2006 to December 2008. Data on postoperative morbidity, in-patient mortality, and cost of hospitalization (as estimated from data on hospital charges) were collected and analyzed. Seventy-three patients received regional anesthesia and 235 patients received general anesthesia. During July 2007, approximately halfway through the study period, a regional anesthesia and analgesia program was introduced. The average cost of hospitalization in patients who received surgery for hip fracture was no different between patients who receive regional or general anesthesia ($16,789 + 631 vs $16,815 + 643, respectively, P = 0.9557). Delay in surgery and intensive care unit (ICU) admission resulted in significantly higher hospitalization costs. Age, male gender, African American race and ICU admission were associated with increased in-hospital mortality. In-hospital mortality and rates of readmission are not statistically different between the two anesthesia groups.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that geriatric patients undergoing hip fracture surgery who receive regional anesthesia will exhibit improved functional recovery and reduced risk of long-term mortality compared to those receiving general anesthesia, as measured by a significant difference in the number of patients achieving full mobility and surviving at one-year follow-up.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that there is no significant difference in the average cost of hospitalization between patients who received regional anesthesia and those who received general anesthesia for hip fracture surgery. Additionally, factors such as delay in surgery, ICU admission, age, male gender, African American race, and ICU admission are associated with higher hospitalization costs or increased in-hospital mortality. The study also found no statistical difference in in-hospital mortality and readmission rates between the two anesthesia groups.

GAP: This study leaves unanswered the question of whether the type of anesthesia affects the long-term outcomes, such as functional recovery, quality of life, and mortality rates beyond the hospital stay, in geriatric patients undergoing hip fracture surgery. It also does not explore the potential benefits of regional anesthesia in reducing postoperative complications and improving patient outcomes in specific subgroups, such as those with certain comorbidities.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that geriatric patients undergoing hip fracture surgery who receive regional anesthesia will exhibit improved functional recovery and reduced risk of long-term mortality compared to those receiving general anesthesia, as measured by a significant difference in the number of patients achieving full mobility and surviving at one-year follow-up.

abstract: To determine whether there are differences between blood pressure (BP) measured by the nurse (NBP), BP measured by the physician (PBP) and self-measured BP in treated hypertensive patients and, if found, to evaluate their clinical importance. An observational study is carried out with hypertensive patients recruited from two village-based community health centres in Catalonia (Spain) serving an area with a total population of 2800 inhabitants. All patients treated for hypertension visiting the health centre on a specific day of the week and during the same timetable between October 2000 and May 2001 were included. The difference between physician-systolic BP and nurse-systolic BP was 5.16 mmHg (95% CI 2.62-7.7; p<0.001). The difference between physician-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP was 4.67 mmHg (95% CI 0.89-8.44; p=0.016). The differences between nurse-systolic BP and self-measured systolic BP were not significant (0.49 mmHg; 95% CI 3.71-2.71; p=0.758). With regards to diastolic BP, no significant differences were found between the different ways of measurement. NBP gave the following values: sensitivity (Sn) of 92% and specificity (Sp) of 60%; positive predictive value (PPV) of 65.7% and negative predictive value (NPV) of 90% with a positive coefficient of probability (CP+) of 2.3 and a negative coefficient of probability (CP-) of 0.133. PBP gave the following results: Sn=72%; Sp=66.7%; PPV=64.3%; NPV=74.1%; CP+=2.16 and CP- = 0.420.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that standardized training of healthcare professionals in blood pressure measurement techniques, focusing on minimizing variability in cuff placement, deflation rate, and patient preparation, will reduce the discrepancies between physician-measured, nurse-measured, and self-measured blood pressure readings in treated hypertensive patients.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract indicate significant differences in systolic blood pressure measurements between physician-measured and nurse-measured blood pressure (5.16 mmHg difference), and between physician-measured and self-measured blood pressure (4.67 mmHg difference), but no significant differences in diastolic blood pressure measurements or between nurse-measured and self-measured systolic blood pressure. The study also reports on the sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and probability coefficients for nurse-measured and physician-measured blood pressure.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of what factors contribute to the observed differences in blood pressure measurements between healthcare professionals and patients themselves, and whether these differences have a significant impact on the management and treatment outcomes of hypertensive patients. Specifically, the study does not investigate how the technique, experience, or patient- healthcare provider interaction might influence the blood pressure measurements.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that standardized training of healthcare professionals in blood pressure measurement techniques, focusing on minimizing variability in cuff placement, deflation rate, and patient preparation, will reduce the discrepancies between physician-measured, nurse-measured, and self-measured blood pressure readings in treated hypertensive patients.

abstract: Earlier studies have demonstrated low peak oxygen uptake ((.)Vo(2)peak) in children with spina bifida. Low peak heart rate and low peak respiratory exchange ratio in these studies raised questions regarding the true maximal character of (.)Vo(2)peak values obtained with treadmill testing. The aim of this study was to determine whether the Vo(2)peak measured during an incremental treadmill test is a true reflection of the maximum oxygen uptake ((.)Vo(2)max) in children who have spina bifida and are ambulatory. A cross-sectional design was used for this study. Twenty children who had spina bifida and were ambulatory participated. The (.)Vo(2)peak was measured during a graded treadmill exercise test. The validity of (.)Vo(2)peak measurements was evaluated by use of previously described guidelines for maximum exercise testing in children who are healthy, as well as differences between Vo(2)peak and (.)Vo(2) during a supramaximal protocol ((.)Vo(2)supramaximal). The average values for (.)Vo(2)peak and normalized (.)Vo(2)peak were, respectively, 1.23 L/min (SD=0.6) and 34.1 mL/kg/min (SD=8.3). Fifteen children met at least 2 of the 3 previously described criteria; one child failed to meet any criteria. Although there were no significant differences between (.)Vo(2)peak and Vo(2)supramaximal, 5 children did show improvement during supramaximal testing. These results apply to children who have spina bifida and are at least community ambulatory.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with spina bifida who exhibit higher muscle oxidative capacity, as measured by phosphorus-31 magnetic resonance spectroscopy, will demonstrate a greater increase in oxygen uptake during supramaximal testing compared to those with lower muscle oxidative capacity.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that in children with spina bifida who are ambulatory, the average peak oxygen uptake (Vo2peak) measured during a graded treadmill exercise test was 1.23 L/min, with 15 out of 20 children meeting at least two criteria for maximum exercise testing, suggesting that Vo2peak may be a valid measure of maximum oxygen uptake (Vo2max) in this population. However, 5 children showed improvement in oxygen uptake during supramaximal testing, indicating potential for further increase in oxygen uptake beyond the initial Vo2peak measurement.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of what specific physiological or functional factors contribute to the variation in Vo2peak and the ability to further increase oxygen uptake during supramaximal testing among children with spina bifida. It also does not explore how these findings might relate to long-term health outcomes or the design of personalized exercise programs for these children.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that children with spina bifida who exhibit higher muscle oxidative capacity, as measured by phosphorus-31 magnetic resonance spectroscopy, will demonstrate a greater increase in oxygen uptake during supramaximal testing compared to those with lower muscle oxidative capacity.

abstract: To compare the probability of prenatal diagnosis (PND) and termination of pregnancy for fetal anomaly (TOPFA) between fetuses conceived by assisted reproductive techniques (ART) and spontaneously-conceived fetuses with congenital heart defects (CHD). Population-based observational study. Paris and surrounding suburbs. Fetuses with CHD in the Paris registry of congenital malformations and cohort of children with CHD (Epicard). Comparison of ART-conceived and spontaneously conceived fetuses taking into account potential confounders (maternal characteristics, multiplicity and year of birth or TOPFA). Probability and gestational age at PND and TOPFA for ART-conceived versus spontaneously conceived fetuses. The probability of PND (28.1% versus 34.6%, P = 0.077) and TOPFA (36.2% versus 39.2%, P = 0.677) were not statistically different between ART-conceived (n = 171) and spontaneously conceived (n = 4620) fetuses. Estimates were similar after adjustment for potential confounders. Gestational age at PND tended to be earlier for ART fetuses (23.1 versus 24.8 weeks, P = 0.05) but no statistical difference was found after adjustment for confounders. Gestational age at TOPFA was comparable between ART-conceived and spontaneously conceived fetuses.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that ART-conceived fetuses with congenital heart defects will exhibit a higher incidence of severe CHD subtypes, such as hypoplastic left heart syndrome, compared to spontaneously conceived fetuses, which could potentially explain the observed tendency towards earlier gestational age at PND in the ART group.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that the probabilities of prenatal diagnosis (PND) and termination of pregnancy for fetal anomaly (TOPFA) are not statistically different between fetuses conceived by assisted reproductive techniques (ART) and spontaneously conceived fetuses with congenital heart defects (CHD). However, there was a tendency for earlier gestational age at PND in ART-conceived fetuses, although this difference disappeared after adjusting for confounders.

GAP: The study does not investigate the underlying factors that might influence the tendency towards earlier PND in ART-conceived fetuses, nor does it explore potential differences in the types of congenital heart defects or their severity between ART and spontaneously conceived fetuses.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that ART-conceived fetuses with congenital heart defects will exhibit a higher incidence of severe CHD subtypes, such as hypoplastic left heart syndrome, compared to spontaneously conceived fetuses, which could potentially explain the observed tendency towards earlier gestational age at PND in the ART group.

abstract: Ablation of persistent atrial fibrillation (AF) may require adjunctive methods of substrate modification. Both ablation-targeting complex fractionated atrial electrograms (CFAEs) recorded during AF and fractionated electrograms recorded during sinus rhythm (sinus rhythm fractionation [SRF]) have been described. However, the relationship of CFAEs with SRF is unclear. Twenty patients (age 62 ± 9 years, 13 males) with persistent AF and 9 control subjects without organic heart disease or AF (age 36 ± 6 years, 4 males) underwent detailed CFAE and SRF left atrial electroanatomic maps. The overlap in left atrial regions with CFAEs and SRF was compared in the AF population, and the distribution of SRF was compared among patients with AF and normal controls. Propagation maps were analyzed to identify the activation patterns associated with SR fractionation. SRF (338 ± 150 points) and CFAE (418 ± 135 points) regions comprised 29% ± 14% and 25% ± 15% of the left atrial surface area, respectively. There was no significant correlation between SRF and CFAE maps (r = .2; P = NS). On comparing patients with AF and controls, no significant difference was found in the distribution of SRF between groups (P = .74). Regions of SRF overlapped areas of wave-front collision 75% ± 13% of the time.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted ablation of left atrial regions exhibiting high frequencies of wave-front collision, as identified by propagation maps during sinus rhythm, will significantly reduce the recurrence of atrial fibrillation in patients undergoing ablation therapy for persistent AF.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract include: (1) the lack of significant correlation between complex fractionated atrial electrograms (CFAEs) and sinus rhythm fractionation (SRF) maps in patients with persistent atrial fibrillation (AF), (2) no significant difference in the distribution of SRF between patients with AF and controls, and (3) regions of SRF often overlap areas of wave-front collision.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of how the specific patterns of wave-front collision in areas of SRF contribute to the initiation or maintenance of atrial fibrillation, and whether modifying these patterns could prevent or reduce the incidence of AF.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that targeted ablation of left atrial regions exhibiting high frequencies of wave-front collision, as identified by propagation maps during sinus rhythm, will significantly reduce the recurrence of atrial fibrillation in patients undergoing ablation therapy for persistent AF.

abstract: Patients living in rural areas may be at a disadvantage in accessing tertiary health care.AIM: To test the hypothesis that very premature infants born to mothers residing in rural areas have poorer outcomes than those residing in urban areas in the state of New South Wales (NSW) and the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) despite a coordinated referral and transport system. "Rural" or "urban" status was based on the location of maternal residence. Perinatal characteristics, major morbidity and case mix adjusted mortality were compared between 1879 rural and 6775 urban infants<32 weeks gestational age, born in 1992-2002 and admitted to all 10 neonatal intensive care units in NSW and ACT. Rural mothers were more likely to be teenaged, indigenous, and to have had a previous premature birth, prolonged ruptured membrane, and antenatal corticosteroid. Urban mothers were more likely to have had assisted conception and a caesarean section. More urban (93% v 83%) infants were born in a tertiary obstetric hospital. Infants of rural residence had a higher mortality (adjusted odds ratio (OR) 1.26, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.07 to 1.48, p = 0.005). This trend was consistently seen in all subgroups and significantly for the tertiary hospital born population and the 30-31 weeks gestation subgroup. Regional birth data in this gestational age range also showed a higher stillbirth rate among rural infants (OR 1.20, 95% CI 1.09 to 1.32, p<0.001).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that rural very premature infants will exhibit increased rates of developmental delays and cognitive impairments at 5 years of age, compared to their urban counterparts, due to differences in access to early intervention services and follow-up care.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that very premature infants born to mothers residing in rural areas have poorer outcomes, including higher mortality rates, compared to those residing in urban areas in NSW and ACT, despite a coordinated referral and transport system. Specifically, rural mothers were more likely to have had previous premature births, prolonged ruptured membranes, and antenatal corticosteroid, while urban mothers were more likely to have had assisted conception and caesarean sections. The study also found that a higher percentage of urban infants were born in tertiary obstetric hospitals, and that rural infants had a higher stillbirth rate.

GAP: The study identifies a disparity in outcomes between rural and urban areas, but it does not explore the specific mechanisms or factors that contribute to this disparity, such as the role of healthcare access, socioeconomic status, or environmental factors. Additionally, the study does not examine the long-term effects of premature birth on rural infants, beyond the initial mortality and morbidity rates.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that rural very premature infants will exhibit increased rates of developmental delays and cognitive impairments at 5 years of age, compared to their urban counterparts, due to differences in access to early intervention services and follow-up care.

abstract: The present study aims to compare strength, healing, and operation time of experimental intestinal anastomoses performed by polyglactin 910 (Vicryl; Ethicon, Edinburgh, United Kingdom) sutures with ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue (Pattex; Henkel, Dusseldorf, Germany). Ninety-six Sprague-Dawley rats were divided into 2 (groups E and L). Each group was further subdivided into 6 subgroups (EA1, EA2, EA3, EB1, EB2, EB3, LA1, LA2, LA3, LB1, LB2, LB3), each containing 8 rats. Intestinal anastomosis was performed by polyglactin 910 sutures in A subgroups and with ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate in B subgroups. The anastomosis was end to end in A1 and B1, side to side in A2 and B2, and end to side in A3 and B3. Time for anastomosis performance (AT) was recorded. In group E, bursting pressures and hydroxyproline levels were determined on the second postoperative day, whereas in group L, the same measurements were made on the sixth postoperative day. One-way analysis of variance was used for analyses of variance in the groups. Quantitative data were analyzed with Student's t test. P value was considered significant at less than .05. There was no significant difference between bursting pressures of subgroup pairs on both postoperative days 2 and 6. Hydroxyproline levels and AT were significantly better in B subgroups.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue for intestinal anastomosis will result in a significant reduction in adhesion formation and improved collagen
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study are that there is no significant difference in bursting pressures between intestinal anastomoses performed with polyglactin 910 sutures and those performed with ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue on both postoperative days 2 and 6. However, hydroxyproline levels and anastomosis time (AT) were significantly better in the groups where ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue was used, indicating potentially faster healing and more efficient surgical procedures with this method.

GAP: The study compares the immediate effects and short-term outcomes of using polyglactin 910 sutures versus ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue for intestinal anastomosis but does not investigate the long-term durability, potential complications, or the histological differences in healing between these two methods. Additionally, it does not explore the mechanisms behind the improved hydroxyproline levels and reduced anastomosis time observed with ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the use of ethyl-2-cyanoacrylate glue for intestinal anastomosis will result in a significant reduction in adhesion formation and improved collagen

abstract: To compare the primary stability of miniscrews inserted into bone blocks of different bone mineral densities (BMDs) with and without cortical bone, and investigate whether some trabecular properties could influence primary stability. Fifty-two bone blocks were extracted from fresh bovine pelvic bone. Four groups were created based on bone type (iliac or pubic region) and presence or absence of cortical bone. Specimens were micro-computed tomography imaged to evaluate trabecular thickness, trabecular number, trabecular separation, bone volume density (BV/TV), BMD, and cortical thickness. Miniscrews 1.4 mm in diameter and 6 mm long were inserted into the bone blocks, and primary stability was evaluated by insertion torque (IT), mini-implant mobility (PTV), and pull-out strength (PS). Intergroup comparison showed lower levels of primary stability when the BMD of trabecular bone was lower and in the absence of cortical bone (P≤.05). The Pearson correlation test showed correlation between trabecular number, trabecular thickness, BV/TV, trabecular BMD, total BMD, and IT, PTV, and PS. There was correlation between cortical thickness and IT and PS (P≤.05).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the angle of miniscrew insertion will significantly interact with bone mineral density and cortical bone presence to influence primary stability, such that insertions at angles greater than 30 degrees to the perpendicular of the bone surface will result in decreased pull-out strength in low-density trabecular bone without cortical support.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The primary stability of miniscrews is influenced by bone mineral density (BMD) and the presence of cortical bone, with lower BMD and absence of cortical bone resulting in lower primary stability. Additionally, trabecular properties such as trabecular number, trabecular thickness, bone volume density, and trabecular BMD, as well as cortical thickness, correlate with insertion torque, mini-implant mobility, and pull-out strength.

GAP: While the study establishes correlations between various bone properties and primary stability of miniscrews, it does not investigate how these correlations might change or be affected by the angle of miniscrew insertion, which could potentially impact the distribution of forces and stability within the bone.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the angle of miniscrew insertion will significantly interact with bone mineral density and cortical bone presence to influence primary stability, such that insertions at angles greater than 30 degrees to the perpendicular of the bone surface will result in decreased pull-out strength in low-density trabecular bone without cortical support.

abstract: The "July effect" refers to the phenomenon of adverse impacts on patient care arising from the changeover in medical staff that takes place during this month at academic medical centres in North America. There has been some evidence supporting the presence of the July effect, including data from surgical specialties. Uniformity of care, regardless of time of year, is required for patients undergoing major cancer surgery. We therefore sought to perform a population-level assessment for the presence of a July effect in this field. We used the Nationwide Inpatient Sample to abstract data on patients undergoing 1 of 8 major cancer surgeries at academic medical centres between Jan. 1, 1999, and Dec. 30, 2009. The primary outcomes examined were postoperative complications and in-hospital mortality. Univariate analyses and subsequently multivariate analyses, controlling for patient and hospital characteristics, were performed to identify whether the time of surgery was an independent predictor of outcome after major cancer surgery. On univariate analysis, the overall postoperative complication rate, as well as genitourinary and hematologic complications specifically, was higher in July than the rest of the year. However, on multivariate analysis, only hematologic complications were significantly higher in July, with no difference in overall postoperative complication rate or in-hospital mortality for all 8 surgeries considered separately or together.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the increased rate of hematologic complications in July for patients undergoing major cancer surgery is directly correlated with the number of new, inexperienced residents participating in the surgical team, and that targeted training and mentoring programs for these residents can significantly reduce the incidence of such complications during the July transition period.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that on univariate analysis, the overall postoperative complication rate and specific types of complications (genitourinary and hematologic) were higher in July than the rest of the year for patients undergoing major cancer surgery at academic medical centers. However, on multivariate analysis, only hematologic complications remained significantly higher in July, with no difference in overall postoperative complication rate or in-hospital mortality.

GAP: The study identifies an increased rate of hematologic complications in July for patients undergoing major cancer surgery but does not explore the underlying causes of this increase or whether specific factors related to the transition of medical staff contribute to this phenomenon. The study also does not examine whether the experience level of the medical staff or the specific dynamics of the handover process in July contribute to the observed increase in hematologic complications.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the increased rate of hematologic complications in July for patients undergoing major cancer surgery is directly correlated with the number of new, inexperienced residents participating in the surgical team, and that targeted training and mentoring programs for these residents can significantly reduce the incidence of such complications during the July transition period.

abstract: Little is known about whether a routinely inserted endoscopic nasobiliary drainage (ENBD) tube improves the clinical course in patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis after clearance of choledocholithiasis. The aim of this study was to investigate the need for ENBD on the clinical outcomes of patients with acute cholangitis undergoing endoscopic clearance of common bile duct (CBD) stones. Prospective, randomized study. Tertiary referral center. A total of 104 patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis who underwent primary endoscopic treatment were compared according to insertion of an ENBD tube (51 in the ENBD group and 53 in the no-ENBD group). Insertion of an ENBD tube after clearance of CBD stones. Recurrence of cholangitis and length of hospital stay after clearance of CBD stones. Baseline clinical characteristics were similar between both groups. There were no significant differences in the recurrence rate of cholangitis at 24 weeks (3.9% for the ENBD group vs 3.8% for the no-ENBD group at 24 weeks; P = .99) and length of hospital stay (7.9 days [standard error = 1.2] for the ENBD group vs 7.9 days [standard error = 0.7]for the no-ENBD group; P = .98). However, procedure time was longer (26.2 [SE = 1.8] minutes vs 22.7 [SE = 1.0]minutes, respectively; P = .01) and the discomfort score was higher (4.9 [SE = 0.4] vs 2.8 [SE = 0.3], respectively; P = .02) in the ENBD group than in the no-ENBD group. Single-center study.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis and elevated bilirubin levels above 5 mg/dL will have a reduced recurrence rate of cholangitis at 6 months when an ENBD tube is inserted after clearance of CBD stones, compared to those without an ENBD tube.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that the insertion of an endoscopic nasobiliary drainage (ENBD) tube after clearance of common bile duct (CBD) stones in patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis does not significantly reduce the recurrence rate of cholangitis or length of hospital stay compared to patients without an ENBD tube. However, the procedure time was longer and the discomfort score was higher in the ENBD group.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of whether the benefits of ENBD tube insertion might be more pronounced in specific subgroups of patients, such as those with more severe cholangitis or underlying biliary tract abnormalities, and what the long-term effects of ENBD tube insertion are on biliary function and patient quality of life.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients with choledocholithiasis-induced acute cholangitis and elevated bilirubin levels above 5 mg/dL will have a reduced recurrence rate of cholangitis at 6 months when an ENBD tube is inserted after clearance of CBD stones, compared to those without an ENBD tube.

abstract: To assess quality of storage of vaccines in the community. Questionnaire survey of general practices and child health clinics, and monitoring of storage temperatures of selected refrigerators. Central Manchester and Bradford health districts. 45 general practices and five child health clinics, of which 40 (80%) responded. Eight practices were selected for refrigeration monitoring. Adherence to Department of Health guidelines for vaccine storage, temperature range to which vaccines were exposed over two weeks. Of the 40 respondents, only 16 were aware of the appropriate storage conditions for the vaccines; eight had minimum and maximum thermometers but only one of these was monitored daily. In six of the eight practices selected for monitoring of refrigeration temperatures the vaccines were exposed to either subzero temperatures (three fridges) or temperatures up to 16 degrees C (three). Two of these were specialised drug storage refrigerators with an incorporated thermostat and external temperature gauges.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that vaccines stored in refrigerators with fluctuating temperatures outside the recommended range (2-8 degrees C) will exhibit a significant reduction in their immunogenicity, as measured by antibody response in recipients, compared to vaccines stored within the recommended temperature range.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that many healthcare providers are unaware of the appropriate storage conditions for vaccines, and even when they have the necessary equipment, such as thermometers, these are often not monitored regularly. Furthermore, the monitoring of refrigeration temperatures in selected practices showed that vaccines were frequently exposed to inappropriate temperatures, either below 0 degrees C or above the recommended range, which could affect their potency.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of how the exposure to inappropriate storage temperatures affects the actual efficacy of the vaccines. It identifies a problem with storage practices but does not investigate the consequences of these practices on vaccine effectiveness.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that vaccines stored in refrigerators with fluctuating temperatures outside the recommended range (2-8 degrees C) will exhibit a significant reduction in their immunogenicity, as measured by antibody response in recipients, compared to vaccines stored within the recommended temperature range.

abstract: Clinically positive axillary nodes are widely considered a contraindication to sentinel lymph node (SLN) biopsy in breast cancer, yet no data support this mandate. In fact, data from the era of axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) suggest that clinical examination of the axilla is falsely positive in as many as 30% of cases. Here we report the results of SLN biopsy in a selected group of breast cancer patients with palpable axillary nodes classified as either moderately or highly suspicious for metastasis. Among 2,027 consecutive SLN biopsy procedures performed by two experienced surgeons, clinically suspicious axillary nodes were identified in 106, and categorized as group 1 (asymmetric enlargement of the ipsilateral axillary nodes moderately suspicious for metastasis, n = 62) and group 2 (clinically positive axillary nodes highly suspicious for metastasis, n = 44). Clinical examination of the axilla was inaccurate in 41% of patients (43 of 106) overall, and was falsely positive in 53% of patients (33 of 62) with moderately suspicious nodes and 23% of patients (10 of 44) with highly suspicious nodes. False-positive results were less frequent with larger tumor size (p = 0.002) and higher histologic grade (p = 0.002), but were not associated with age, body mass index, or a previous surgical biopsy.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the expression of specific tumor-associated biomarkers, such as those involved in immune evasion or angiogenesis, will be significantly altered in breast cancer patients with larger tumor size and higher histologic grade, leading to a decreased likelihood of false-positive clinical examination results.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that clinical examination of the axilla is inaccurate in 41% of patients with breast cancer and palpable axillary nodes, with false-positive results occurring in 53% of patients with moderately suspicious nodes and 23% of patients with highly suspicious nodes. Additionally, false-positive results were less frequent with larger tumor size and higher histologic grade, but were not associated with age, body mass index, or previous surgical biopsy.

GAP: The study does not investigate the underlying biological mechanisms that contribute to the discrepancy between clinical examination and actual metastasis in axillary nodes, particularly in relation to tumor characteristics such as size and histologic grade.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the expression of specific tumor-associated biomarkers, such as those involved in immune evasion or angiogenesis, will be significantly altered in breast cancer patients with larger tumor size and higher histologic grade, leading to a decreased likelihood of false-positive clinical examination results.

abstract: The prevalence of retinal haemorrhages after convulsions is not well established. As these haemorrhages are considered characteristic of child abuse, we investigated their occurrence after convulsive episodes to see whether the finding of haemorrhage should prompt further investigation. Prospective study of 153 children (aged 2 months to 2 years), seen in the emergency department after a convulsive episode. After a thorough history and physical examination, a retinal examination was performed by an ophthalmologist. If findings were positive, further investigation was undertaken to rule out systemic disorder or child abuse. One child was found with unilateral retinal haemorrhages following an episode of a simple febrile convulsion. A thorough investigation uncovered no other reason for this finding.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the occurrence of retinal haemorrhages in children after convulsive episodes is associated with the intensity and duration of the convulsion, such that children experiencing more severe or prolonged convulsions will have a higher incidence of retinal haemorrhages compared to those with milder or shorter convulsions.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that out of 153 children aged 2 months to 2 years who experienced convulsive episodes, only one child was found with unilateral retinal haemorrhages following a simple febrile convulsion, and thorough investigation uncovered no other reason for this finding, suggesting that retinal haemorrhages can occur in the absence of child abuse or systemic disorder in this context.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of the underlying mechanisms by which retinal haemorrhages may occur in children after convulsive episodes without any evidence of child abuse or systemic disorder, and whether there are specific factors that predispose certain children to this condition.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the occurrence of retinal haemorrhages in children after convulsive episodes is associated with the intensity and duration of the convulsion, such that children experiencing more severe or prolonged convulsions will have a higher incidence of retinal haemorrhages compared to those with milder or shorter convulsions.

abstract: Community-based medical education is growing to meet the increased demand for quality clinical education in expanded settings, and its sustainability relies on patient participation. This study investigated patients' views on being used as an educational resource for teaching medical students. Questionnaire-based survey. Patients attending six rural and 11 regional general practices in New South Wales over 18 teaching sessions in November 2008, who consented to student involvement in their consultation. Patient perceptions, expectations and acceptance of medical student involvement in consultations, assessed by surveys before and after their consultations. 118 of 122 patients consented to medical student involvement; of these, 117 (99%) completed a survey before the consultation, and 100 (85%) after the consultation. Patients were overwhelmingly positive about their doctor and practice being involved in student teaching and felt they themselves played an important role. Pre-consultation, patients expressed reluctance to allow students to conduct some or all aspects of the consultation independently. However, after the consultation, they reported they would have accepted higher levels of involvement than actually occurred.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients who are provided with clear explanations of the benefits and expectations of medical student involvement in their care will exhibit a greater increase in acceptance of higher levels of student involvement compared to those who do not receive such explanations.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that patients are overwhelmingly positive about their doctor and practice being involved in student teaching, and they feel they play an important role in medical education. Despite initial reluctance, patients become more accepting of medical students' involvement in consultations after the experience, with a majority willing to accept higher levels of student involvement than they initially anticipated.

GAP: The study does not explore the specific factors that contribute to the change in patients' attitudes towards medical student involvement in consultations from before to after the experience. It also does not examine how the level of patient acceptance of medical student involvement affects the educational outcomes for the students or the quality of care provided to the patients.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients who are provided with clear explanations of the benefits and expectations of medical student involvement in their care will exhibit a greater increase in acceptance of higher levels of student involvement compared to those who do not receive such explanations.

abstract: This study aims to study femoral tunnel lengths drilled with a flexible reamer and the distance to important lateral structures obtained by flexing the knee at various angles and by drilling the guide pins arthroscopically to resemble clinical practice. The purpose of this cadaveric study was twofold: 1. to determine whether femoral tunnel lengths of greater than 20 mm can be created with a flexible reamer system at 90 ° of knee flexion and 2. to determine whether the lateral structures of the knee are safe with this technique. Ten fresh cadaveric knees were utilized. The intra-osseous length can be measured with a specially de - signed flexible guide pin. Flexible pins were inserted with the knee at 70°, 90°, and 120° of flexion. The intra-osseous length was measured with the measuring device. Each speci - men was dissected around the lateral aspect of the knee to identify the critical structures, the common peroneal nerve, and the LCL. The distance from the guide pins to the com - mon peroneal nerve and femoral attachment of the LCL were measured with a standard flexible paper ruler to the nearest millimeter. There is a trend for progressively increasing mean intra-osseous length associated with increased flexion of the knee. The mean intra-osseous length for 70° flexion was 25.2 mm (20 mm to 32 mm), which was statistically significant when compared to mean intra-osseous lengths of 32.1 mm (22 mm to 45 mm) and 38.0 mm (34 mm to 45 mm) in the 90° and 120° flexion groups, respectively (p<0.05). There were no significant differences among the groups with respect to distance to the LCL. There is a trend toward longer distances to the common peroneal nerve with increased flexion. There was a statistically significant dif - ference when comparing 120° versus 70° (p<0.05).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that ACL reconstructions performed using a flexible reamer system with knee flexion angles of 120° will result in improved graft stability and reduced risk of complications, such as common peroneal nerve damage, compared to reconstructions performed at 70° of knee flexion, as measured by clinical outcomes and
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that using a flexible reamer system to drill femoral tunnels at 90° of knee flexion can create tunnel lengths of greater than 20 mm, and that there is a trend for progressively increasing mean intra-osseous length associated with increased flexion of the knee. Additionally, the study found that the distance to the common peroneal nerve increases with increased flexion, with a statistically significant difference between 120° and 70° of flexion. The study also found no significant differences among the groups with respect to distance to the LCL.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of how the observed trends in intra-osseous length and distance to critical structures at different knee flexion angles affect the long-term stability and functional outcomes of knee surgeries, such as anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) reconstructions, that utilize flexible reamer systems for femoral tunnel creation.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that ACL reconstructions performed using a flexible reamer system with knee flexion angles of 120° will result in improved graft stability and reduced risk of complications, such as common peroneal nerve damage, compared to reconstructions performed at 70° of knee flexion, as measured by clinical outcomes and

abstract: As part of a prospective study on quality of life in newly diagnosed lung cancer patients an investigation was carried out to examine whether there were differences among patients' quality of life scores and their socioeconomic status. Quality of life was measured at two points in time (baseline and three months after initial treatment) using three standard instruments; the Nottingham Health Profile (NHP), the European Organization for Research and Cancer Treatment Quality of Life Questionnaire (EORTC QLQ-C30) and its lung cancer supplement (QLQ-LC13). Socioeconomic status for each individual patient was derived using Carstairs and Morris Deprivation Category ranging from 1 (least deprived) to 7 (most deprived) on the basis of the postcode sector of their address. In all, 129 lung cancer patients entered into the study. Of these data for 82 patients were complete (at baseline and follow-up). 57% of patients were of lower socioeconomic status and they had more health problems, less functioning, and more symptoms as compared to affluent patients. Of these, physical mobility (P = 0.05), energy (P = 0.01), role functioning (P = 0.04), physical functioning (P = 0.03), and breathlessness (P = 0.02) were significant at baseline.
