abstract: Suicide is a leading cause of death and has been strongly associated with affective disorders. The influence of affective disorder polarity on subsequent suicide attempts or completions and any differential effect of suicide risk factors by polarity were assessed in a prospective cohort. Participants with major affective disorders in the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) Collaborative Depression Study (CDS) were followed prospectively for up to 25 years. A total of 909 participants meeting prospective diagnostic criteria for major depressive and bipolar disorders were followed through 4204 mood cycles. Suicidal behavior was defined as suicide attempts or completions. Mixed-effects, grouped-time survival analysis assessed risk of suicidal behavior and differential effects of risk factors for suicidal behavior by polarity. In addition to polarity, the main effects of age, gender, hopelessness, married status, prior suicide attempts and active substance abuse were modeled, with mood cycle as the unit of analysis. After controlling for age of onset, there were no differences in prior suicide attempts by polarity although bipolar participants had more prior severe attempts. During follow-up, 40 cycles ended in suicide and 384 cycles contained at least one suicide attempt. Age, hopelessness and active substance abuse but not polarity predicted suicidal behavior. The effects of risk factors did not differ by polarity.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the severity of depressive symptoms, particularly those related to rumination and impulsivity, will be a stronger predictor of suicidal behavior in individuals with major depressive disorder than in those with bipolar disorder, even after controlling for hopelessness, substance abuse, and other established risk factors.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study are that in a prospective cohort of participants with major affective disorders, age, hopelessness, and active substance abuse predicted suicidal behavior, but the polarity of the affective disorder (major depressive vs. bipolar) did not. Additionally, while there were no differences in prior suicide attempts by polarity, bipolar participants had more prior severe attempts. The study also found that the effects of risk factors for suicidal behavior did not differ by polarity.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of whether the specific symptoms or symptom clusters of major depressive and bipolar disorders, rather than just the polarity of the disorder, contribute to the risk of suicidal behavior. The study controlled for several factors but did not examine the role of specific symptoms or their severity in predicting suicidal behavior.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the severity of depressive symptoms, particularly those related to rumination and impulsivity, will be a stronger predictor of suicidal behavior in individuals with major depressive disorder than in those with bipolar disorder, even after controlling for hopelessness, substance abuse, and other established risk factors.

abstract: In an attempt to improve the care they provide for their patients with breast cancer, the authors' institution developed a multidisciplinary breast cancer clinic (MDBCC) to offer "one-stop shopping" consultation and support for newly diagnosed breast cancer patients. One hundred sixty-two patients, the control group for this study, were evaluated at Henry Ford Hospital during the year prior to the opening of the MDBCC. These patients, who were referred in the traditional sequential consultation manner, were compared with the first 177 patients seen during the first year of the clinic's operation. Retrospective chart reviews were conducted to assess treatment timeliness, and anonymous questionnaires were used to assess patient satisfaction. The authors found that the MDBCC increased patient satisfaction by encouraging involvement of patients' families and friends and by helping patients make treatment decisions (P<0.001). The time between diagnosis and the initiation of treatment was also significantly decreased (42.2 days vs. 29.6 days; P<0.0008).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with breast cancer who are treated in a multidisciplinary breast cancer clinic will exhibit improved five-year survival rates compared to those receiving traditional sequential consultation care, due to enhanced timeliness of treatment initiation and increased patient engagement in their care plan.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that the multidisciplinary breast cancer clinic (MDBCC) significantly improved patient satisfaction by facilitating family and friend involvement and aiding in treatment decision-making, and it reduced the time between diagnosis and the start of treatment from 42.2 days to 29.6 days.

GAP: This study leaves unanswered the long-term effects of the MDBCC on patient outcomes, such as survival rates, recurrence rates, and quality of life over an extended period. It also does not explore how the clinic's model could be adapted or improved for patients with different types of cancer or those in various stages of their disease.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients with breast cancer who are treated in a multidisciplinary breast cancer clinic will exhibit improved five-year survival rates compared to those receiving traditional sequential consultation care, due to enhanced timeliness of treatment initiation and increased patient engagement in their care plan.

abstract: As part of an MRC funded study into primary care oral anticoagulation management, INR measurements obtained in general practice were validated against values on the same samples obtained in hospital laboratories. A prospective comparative trial was undertaken between three hospital laboratories and nine general practices. All patients attending general practice based anticoagulant clinics had parallel INR estimations performed in general practice and in a hospital laboratory. 405 tests were performed. Comparison between results obtained in the practices and those in the reference hospital laboratory (gold standard), which used the same method of testing for INR, showed a correlation coefficient of 0.96. Correlation coefficients comparing the results with the various standard laboratory techniques ranged from 0.86 to 0.92. It was estimated that up to 53% of tests would have resulted in clinically significant differences (change in warfarin dose) depending upon the site and method of testing. The practice derived results showed a positive bias ranging from 0.28 to 1.55, depending upon the site and method of testing.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with INR values measured in general practice will have a higher incidence of bleeding complications compared to those with INR values measured in hospital laboratories, due to the positive bias in INR results observed in general practice settings, leading to potentially higher warfarin doses being prescribed.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study include a high correlation coefficient (0.96) between INR measurements obtained in general practice and those obtained in a reference hospital laboratory, as well as correlation coefficients ranging from 0.86 to 0.92 when compared to various standard laboratory techniques. Additionally, the study found that up to 53% of tests could result in clinically significant differences in warfarin dosing depending on the testing site and method, with practice-derived results showing a positive bias ranging from 0.28 to 1.55.

GAP: A significant knowledge gap left unanswered by this study is the clinical impact of these differences in INR measurements on patient outcomes, such as bleeding risks or thromboembolic events, when anticoagulation management is based on INR results from general practice versus hospital laboratory settings.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients with INR values measured in general practice will have a higher incidence of bleeding complications compared to those with INR values measured in hospital laboratories, due to the positive bias in INR results observed in general practice settings, leading to potentially higher warfarin doses being prescribed.

abstract: A cross sectional study of 38 patients fulfilling the classification criteria for SLE. The patients visited a rheumatology outpatient clinic in Switzerland between January 2002 and December 2004. The last assessment during this period was used for the study. The assessment included, besides demographic data, the measurement of disease activity using the BILAG index, the measurement of disease damage using the SLICC/ACR damage index (SDI), as well as the patient's self assessed health status using the patient's questionnaire Medical Outcome Survey Short Form 36 (SF-36). A total of 36 women and 2 men were included in the study (median age: 43 yrs, median disease duration: 11 yrs). Increased disease activity (total BILAG) was shown to be significantly correlated with reduced physical function. A greater damage (total SDI) correlated significantly with reduced role function due to emotional limitations. Neither age nor disease duration showed any significant correlation with health status in this study.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reduction in disease activity, as measured by a decrease in the BILAG index score, will be associated with a significant improvement in physical component summary scores of the SF-36 over a 12-month period in SLE patients.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study are that increased disease activity in SLE patients, as measured by the BILAG index, is significantly correlated with reduced physical function, and greater disease damage, as measured by the SLICC/ACR damage index (SDI), is significantly correlated with reduced role function due to emotional limitations. Additionally, the study found no significant correlation between age, disease duration, and health status in SLE patients.

GAP: This study leaves unanswered the question of whether specific domains of disease activity or damage are more strongly associated with particular aspects of self-assessed health status, such as physical or emotional functioning. It also does not explore potential longitudinal relationships between disease activity, damage, and changes in health status over time.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that a reduction in disease activity, as measured by a decrease in the BILAG index score, will be associated with a significant improvement in physical component summary scores of the SF-36 over a 12-month period in SLE patients.

abstract: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels can show wide fluctuations when repeatedly measured. Here we investigatewd if: (a) biopsy timing influences the prostate cancer (PC) detection rate in patients with fluctuating PSA (flu-PSA) in comparison with patients with steadily increasing PSA (si-PSA); (b) PSA slope estimated in patients with flu-PSA predicts a different risk of cancer detection; (c) flu-PSA and si-PSA patients develop PC in topographically different sites; (d) the behaviour of pre-operative PSA is an expression of a disease with defferent characteristics to the following radical prostatectomy. The study involved 211 patients who underwent at least a second biopsy after a first negative prostate biopsy. PSA Slope, PSA velocity (PSAV) and PSA doubling time (PSADT) were estimated. Flu-PSA level was defined as a PSA series with at least one PSA value lower than the one immediately preceding it. 82 patients had flu-PSA levels and 129 si-PSA levels. There were no significant differences between the two groups in terms of cancer detection, clinical or pathological stage, but the si-PSA group with cancer had a higher Gleason score. No difference was found for PSA Slope between flu-PSA patients with cancer and those without.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that prostate cancers in patients with si-PSA will exhibit a higher frequency of genetic mutations associated with aggressive disease, such as TP53 and PTEN alterations, compared to prostate cancers in patients with flu-PSA, and that these mutations will correlate with the higher Gleason scores observed in the si-PSA group.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that patients with fluctuating PSA (flu-PSA) levels and those with steadily increasing PSA (si-PSA) levels did not differ significantly in terms of cancer detection, clinical or pathological stage, but si-PSA patients with cancer had higher Gleason scores. Additionally, no difference was found in PSA slope between flu-PSA patients with cancer and those without. The study involved 211 patients who underwent at least a second biopsy after a first negative prostate biopsy.

GAP: The study does not investigate the potential molecular or pathological differences between prostate cancers arising in patients with flu-PSA and si-PSA, particularly in relation to tumor aggressiveness and genetic mutations. The current findings suggest different disease characteristics but do not explore the underlying biological mechanisms that could explain the observed differences in Gleason scores between the two groups.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that prostate cancers in patients with si-PSA will exhibit a higher frequency of genetic mutations associated with aggressive disease, such as TP53 and PTEN alterations, compared to prostate cancers in patients with flu-PSA, and that these mutations will correlate with the higher Gleason scores observed in the si-PSA group.

abstract: Adoption and implementation of evidence-based measures for catheter care leads to reductions in central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rates in the NICU. The purpose of this study is to evaluate whether this rate reduction is sustainable for at least 1 year and to identify key determinants of this sustainability at the NICU of the Floating Hospital for Children at Tufts Medical Center. We reviewed the incidence of CLABSIs in the NICU temporally to the implementation of new practice policies and procedures, from July 2008 to December 2013. Adoption of standardized care practices, including bundles and checklists, was associated with a significant reduction of the CLABSI rate to zero for>370 consecutive days in our NICU in 2012. Overall, our CLABSI rates decreased from 4.1 per 1000 line days in 2009 (13 infections; 3163 line days) to 0.94 in 2013 (2 infections; 2115 line days), which represents a 77% reduction over a 5-year period. In the first quarter of 2013, there was a brief increase in CLABSI rate to 3.3 per 1000 line days; after a series of interventions, the CLABSI rate was maintained at zero for>600 days. Ongoing training, surveillance, and vigilance with catheter insertion and maintenance practices and improved documentation were identified as key drivers for success.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing the frequency of training sessions on catheter care protocols from quarterly to monthly will result in a significant improvement in staff competency, as measured by adherence to checklist protocols, and consequently lead to a further reduction in CLABSI rates by at least 20% over a 12-month period compared to the current training schedule.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The implementation of evidence-based measures for catheter care in the NICU leads to a significant reduction in central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) rates, with a 77% reduction over a 5-year period, and key drivers for success include ongoing training, surveillance, and vigilance with catheter insertion and maintenance practices, as well as improved documentation, allowing for sustained periods of zero CLABSIs.

GAP: While the study identifies factors contributing to the reduction and sustainability of low CLABSI rates, it does not investigate the specific impact of the frequency and quality of ongoing training on the maintenance of these low infection rates, particularly in relation to the competency of healthcare staff in adhering to the new practice policies and procedures.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that increasing the frequency of training sessions on catheter care protocols from quarterly to monthly will result in a significant improvement in staff competency, as measured by adherence to checklist protocols, and consequently lead to a further reduction in CLABSI rates by at least 20% over a 12-month period compared to the current training schedule.

abstract: Severe upper gastrointestinal (GI) motor disorders, including gastroparesis (GP), can consume significant health care resources. Many patients are refractory to traditional drug therapy. To compare symptoms, healthcare resource utilization and costs in two groups of patients with the symptoms of GP: those treated via gastric electrical stimulation (GES) and those treated with traditional pharmacological agents in an intensive outpatient program (MED). A long-term comparison of patients with devices (n = 9) vs intensive medical therapy (n = 9). A total of 18 eligible patients with the symptoms of GP reported for 1-year baseline and long-term treatment for 3 years. Patients with the symptoms of GP were treated by a GES or intensive medical therapy (MED). GP Symptoms, healthcare resource utilization using investigator-derived independent outcome measure score (IDIOMS) and total hospital (inpatient and outpatient) billing costs. Gastrointestinal symptoms were significantly different from baseline (F = 3.03, P<0.017) with GP patients treated via GES showing more sustained improvement over 36 months than those treated via MED. Healthcare resource usage, measured via the IDIOMS, significantly improved at 12, 24 and 36 month follow-up for GES patients (F = 10.49, P<0.001), compared with patients receiving medical therapy, who demonstrated further deterioration. GP patients treated via GES also proved superior to medical therapy at 24 and 36 months with regard to decreased costs (F = 4.85, P<0.001). Within group comparisons indicated significantly reduced hospital days for both patient groups; however, no statistical differences were noted between groups in terms of hospital days. Three of nine patients in the MED group died primarily from i.v. access related problems; none of the GES patients died.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that gastric electrical stimulation will increase gastric emptying rates and reduce inflammatory markers in the gastrointestinal tract of patients with gastroparesis, compared to traditional medical therapy, thereby contributing to improved symptom management and reduced healthcare resource utilization.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study include: (1) patients with gastroparesis (GP) treated with gastric electrical stimulation (GES) showed more sustained improvement in gastrointestinal symptoms over 36 months compared to those treated with medical therapy (MED), (2) GES patients demonstrated significant improvement in healthcare resource utilization, (3) GES patients had decreased costs at 24 and 36 months, and (4) there were no deaths in the GES group, whereas three patients in the MED group died due to i.v. access related problems.

GAP: The study compared the effectiveness of GES and MED in managing GP symptoms, healthcare resource utilization, and costs, but it did not investigate the potential mechanisms by which GES exerts its beneficial effects on GP symptoms and healthcare outcomes. Specifically, the study did not examine the impact of GES on gastric motility, inflammation, or other physiological parameters that may be relevant to GP.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that gastric electrical stimulation will increase gastric emptying rates and reduce inflammatory markers in the gastrointestinal tract of patients with gastroparesis, compared to traditional medical therapy, thereby contributing to improved symptom management and reduced healthcare resource utilization.

abstract: Seventeen patients, aged 7-54 years (mean of 37.2 year), were treated by pulpotomy and filling with ProRoot MTA(®)  in premolar or molar teeth with vital pulps and without clinical evidence of irreversible pulpitis. The patients were then followed up for 12 to 24 months and the teeth then assessed by clinical and radiographic examination. Statistical analysis was performed with Kaplan-Meier survival probability statistics to estimate the survival of the treated teeth. At 24 months, the survival rate without any complementary treatment was estimated to be 82%. Two of the 17 treated teeth required root canal treatment for pain control and one for prosthetic reasons.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that younger patients (aged 7-18 years) will exhibit a significantly higher survival rate of treated teeth without complementary treatment compared to older patients (aged 37-54 years) when undergoing pulpotomy and filling with ProRoot MTA, due to potential differences in pulp biology and healing capacity.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that treating vital pulps in premolar or molar teeth with pulpotomy and filling with ProRoot MTA resulted in an 82% survival rate without additional treatment over a 24-month period, with some teeth requiring root canal treatment or prosthetic intervention.

GAP: The study does not investigate the factors that contribute to the 18% failure rate, such as the role of patient age, tooth type, or specific procedural variables in determining the success of the treatment.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that younger patients (aged 7-18 years) will exhibit a significantly higher survival rate of treated teeth without complementary treatment compared to older patients (aged 37-54 years) when undergoing pulpotomy and filling with ProRoot MTA, due to potential differences in pulp biology and healing capacity.

abstract: Cross-sectional. To identify the regional and global apexes of curves in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and to compare the levels of those with the most rotated vertebral levels on computed tomography scans. The terminology regarding the terms and definitions had been arbitrary until being refined and standardized by the Scoliosis Research Society Working Group on Three-Dimensional Terminology of Spinal Deformity. Apical vertebra or disc is defined as the most laterally deviated vertebra or disc in a scoliosis curve, but the most rotated vertebra (or disc) has not been included in this terminology. One study suggested that the most rotated vertebral level was always located at the apex. Thirty-three structural curves of 25 consecutive patients scheduled for surgery for thoracic or thoracolumbar scoliosis were analyzed with standing anteroposterior radiographs and computed tomography scans covering the curve apexes and pelvis. Thoracic and lumbar curves were evaluated separately for all Type II curves. Vertebral rotations were normalized by the rotation of the pelvis. The most rotated vertebral (or disc) levels (transverse apex) were compared with the regional and global apex levels (vertebra or disc) (coronal apexes) of the corresponding curves separately. Regional and global apexes were at the same level in 18 (54.5%) curves, and within half a level in another 15 (45.4%), and the regional apex was one level higher in two curves (95% confidence levels: -0.82, +0.88). Comparison of the most rotated levels with regional and global apex levels revealed a higher variability, extending up to two levels for the global apex (95% confidence levels: -1.19, +1.54 levels for the global and -1.0, +1.41 levels for the regional apexes).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude of discordance between the most rotated vertebral level and the global apex of the curve will positively correlate with the severity of curvature in adolescent idiopathic scol
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract include the identification of regional and global apexes of curves in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and the comparison of these levels with the most rotated vertebral levels on computed tomography scans. The study found that the regional and global apexes were at the same level in 54.5% of curves and within half a level in another 45.4%, while the comparison of the most rotated levels with regional and global apex levels revealed a higher variability. The most rotated vertebral level was not consistently located at the same level as the regional or global apex, with a variability of up to two levels for the global apex.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of how the variability in the location of the most rotated vertebral level relative to the regional and global apexes affects the progression or treatment outcomes of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis. Specifically, it does not investigate whether the degree of discordance between the most rotated level and the apexes influences the severity of the curvature or the effectiveness of surgical interventions.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the magnitude of discordance between the most rotated vertebral level and the global apex of the curve will positively correlate with the severity of curvature in adolescent idiopathic scol

abstract: This paper assesses the usefulness of the Child Health Computing System as a source of information about children with cerebral palsy. A comparative survey of information held on the Child Health Computing System (CHCS) and the Northern Ireland Cerebral Palsy Register (NICPR) in one Health and Social Services Board in Northern Ireland was carried out. The sample comprised children with cerebral palsy aged 5-9 years. Of the 135 cases recorded on the NICPR, 47 per cent were not found on the CHCS; the majority of these children had no computer record of any medical diagnosis. Of the 82 cases recorded on the CHCS, 10 (12 per cent) were not found on the NICPR; five of these cases (6 per cent) were found on follow-up not to have CP.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that children with cerebral palsy who have more severe motor impairments or more frequent hospitalizations will be more likely to have consistent records across both the CHCS and NICPR, compared to those with milder symptoms or less frequent medical interventions.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that there is a significant discrepancy between the information held on the Child Health Computing System (CHCS) and the Northern Ireland Cerebral Palsy Register (NICPR), with 47% of children with cerebral palsy recorded on the NICPR not found on the CHCS, and 12% of cases recorded on the CHCS not found on the NICPR, including 6% that were later found not to have cerebral palsy.

GAP: This work leaves unanswered the question of what factors contribute to the discrepancies between the CHCS and NICPR, particularly in terms of the characteristics of children with cerebral palsy who are more or less likely to be recorded on one system versus the other, and how these discrepancies might impact the quality of care and outcomes for these children.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that children with cerebral palsy who have more severe motor impairments or more frequent hospitalizations will be more likely to have consistent records across both the CHCS and NICPR, compared to those with milder symptoms or less frequent medical interventions.

abstract: The last 20 years has seen a marked improvement in skin cancer awareness campaigns. We sought to establish whether this has affected the presenting Breslow thickness of malignant melanoma in the South West. This is a retrospective study looking at the first presentation of melanomas from 2003 to 2011. Data was accessed using the local online melanoma database. A total of 2001 new melanomas presented from 2003 to 2012 (Male:Female = 1:1.062). The average yearly number of melanomas was 200.1 (range = 138-312). The mean age was 62.5 years (range 12-99). Data was analysed using a Chi² test. For 0-1 mm melanomas, there is a significant difference in the observed versus expected values over the 10 years (p = 0.0018). There is an increasing proportion of 0-1 mm (thin) melanomas presenting year on year, with a positive linear trend. This is very statistically significant (p<0.0001). The 1-2 mm melanomas are decreasing in proportion with a negative linear trend (p = 0.0013). The 2-4 mm are also decreasing in proportion (p = 0.0253). There is no significant change in the thick>4 mm melanomas (p = 0.1456).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the implementation of targeted skin self-examination training programs, as an adjunct to public awareness campaigns, will lead to a further significant reduction in the mean Breslow thickness of malignant melanomas at presentation, compared to public awareness campaigns alone, within a 5-year follow-up period.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract are that there has been a significant increase in the proportion of thin (0-1 mm) melanomas presenting over the 10-year period, a decrease in the proportion of 1-2 mm and 2-4 mm melanomas, and no significant change in the proportion of thick (>4 mm) melanomas. The data also shows a positive linear trend for thin melanomas and negative linear trends for 1-2 mm and 2-4 mm melanomas.

GAP: The study establishes a correlation between the improvement in skin cancer awareness campaigns and the shift towards thinner melanomas at presentation, but it does not investigate the specific mechanisms or factors contributing to this trend, such as the role of early detection methods, changes in population behavior, or improvements in healthcare access and diagnostic technologies.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the implementation of targeted skin self-examination training programs, as an adjunct to public awareness campaigns, will lead to a further significant reduction in the mean Breslow thickness of malignant melanomas at presentation, compared to public awareness campaigns alone, within a 5-year follow-up period.

abstract: A genetic component is well established in the etiology of breast cancer. It is not well known, however, whether genetic traits also influence prognostic features of the malignant phenotype. We carried out a population-based cohort study in Sweden based on the nationwide Multi-Generation Register. Among all women with breast cancer diagnosed from 1961 to 2001, 2,787 mother-daughter pairs and 831 sister pairs with breast cancer were identified; we achieved complete follow-up and classified 5-year breast cancer-specific prognosis among proband (mother or oldest sister) into tertiles as poor, intermediary, or good. We used Kaplan-Meier estimates of survival proportions and Cox models to calculate relative risks of dying from breast cancer within 5 years depending on the proband's outcome. The 5-year survival proportion among daughters whose mothers died within 5 years was 87% compared to 91% if the mother was alive (p = 0.03). Among sisters, the corresponding proportions were 70% and 88%, respectively (p = 0.001). After adjustment for potential confounders, daughters and sisters of a proband with poor prognosis had a 60% higher 5-year breast cancer mortality compared to those of a proband with good prognosis (hazard ratio [HR], 1.6; 95% confidence interval [CI], 1.2 to 2.2; p for trend 0.002). This association was slightly stronger among sisters (HR, 1.8; 95% CI, 1.0 to 3.4) than among daughters (HR, 1.6; 95% CI, 1.1 to 2.3).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that daughters and sisters of women with breast cancer who had a poor 5-year prognosis will exhibit increased expression of tumor aggressiveness genes, such as those involved in the PI3K/AKT or TP53 pathways, compared to those whose relatives had a good prognosis, and that this difference in gene expression will be associated with a poorer clinical outcome.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that daughters and sisters of women with breast cancer who had a poor 5-year prognosis were at a higher risk of dying from breast cancer within 5 years, with a 60% higher 5-year breast cancer mortality compared to those whose relatives had a good prognosis. Specifically, the 5-year survival proportion was lower among daughters and sisters of probands with poor prognosis, and the association was stronger among sisters than among daughters.

GAP: While the study established an association between the prognosis of relatives and the risk of breast cancer mortality, it does not investigate the specific genetic or molecular mechanisms underlying this association. The study also does not examine whether the prognosis of relatives influences the aggressiveness or biological characteristics of breast cancer in daughters and sisters.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that daughters and sisters of women with breast cancer who had a poor 5-year prognosis will exhibit increased expression of tumor aggressiveness genes, such as those involved in the PI3K/AKT or TP53 pathways, compared to those whose relatives had a good prognosis, and that this difference in gene expression will be associated with a poorer clinical outcome.

abstract: This study was planned to evaluate whether increased nuchal translucency (NT) thickness in the first trimester of gestation can be related to onset of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) during pregnancy. From January 2006 to August 2008, a group of 678 singleton pregnancies who had developed GDM has been selected as a study group among a total of 3966 pregnant women who had undergone first trimester screening for aneuploidies at 11-14 weeks of gestation. A group of 420 single pregnant women with physiological pregnancy were enrolled as control group. Both fetal structural and karyotype's anomalies were excluded in the two groups. NT was mesured by a Fetal Medicine Foundation certificated operator; GDM was diagnosed at 24-28 weeks of gestation following Carpenter and Coustan criteria. In the analyses of continuos variables, study and control group were compared by Student's t-test and Anova test. There was no significative difference (p = 0.585) between NT values in the study (mean = 1.56) and control group (mean = 1.54).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combination of nuchal translucency thickness and other first-trimester maternal serum biomarkers, such as placental growth factor, will be predictive of gestational diabetes mellitus onset in pregnant women.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found no significant difference in nuchal translucency (NT) thickness between women who developed gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and those with physiological pregnancies, suggesting that increased NT thickness in the first trimester may not be a predictor of GDM onset.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of whether other first-trimester markers or factors, potentially related to NT thickness, could be associated with the development of GDM, as the focus was solely on NT thickness.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the combination of nuchal translucency thickness and other first-trimester maternal serum biomarkers, such as placental growth factor, will be predictive of gestational diabetes mellitus onset in pregnant women.

abstract: To examine family-based transmission of the number of 5' flanking arginine vasopressin V1a receptor (AVPR1A) microsatellites, which include [(GATA)(14)] and complex [(CT)(4)-TT-(CT)(8)-(GT)(24)]repeats, in probands with a prepubertal and early adolescent bipolar disorder phenotype (PEA-BP). Preferential transmission of the number of AVPR1A microsatellite repeats to hypersexual and uninhibited people-seeking probands was hypothesized, based on reports from preclinical work in the literature. Probands were 83 participants in an ongoing controlled study of PEA-BP. The PEA-BP phenotype was defined by DSM-IV mania with at least one of the cardinal symptoms of mania (elation and/or grandiosity) to avoid diagnosing mania only by symptoms that overlapped with those for attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Comprehensive assessment of the probands included separate Washington University in St. Louis Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (WASH-U-KSADS) interviews of parents about their children and of children about themselves. Hypersexuality and uninhibited people-seeking were assessed from the corresponding WASH-U-KSADS items. Microsatellite genotyping of the AVPR1A repeats was conducted using fluorescently labeled primers and detected by laser-induced fluorescence. Alleles were determined with the assistance of semi-automated allele-calling software. There were 32 complete, biological trios (28 informative families) for the GATA repeat and 34 complete, biological trios (30 informative families) for the complex repeat. Data were analyzed using case-control and family-based association methods. Preferential transmission of AVPR1A GATA or complex repeats was not significant for hypersexuality or uninhibited people-seeking, using the transmission disequilibrium test. Similarly, case-control analyses found no significant associations between hypersexuality or uninhibited people-seeking and the number of AVPR1A GATA or complex repeats. For p<0.05, there was about 80% power to detect odds ratios of 5.0 and 4.0 (in the family-based analyses) and 3.5 and 2.6 (in the case-control analyses), for allele frequencies of 0.1 and 0.5, respectively.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interaction between AVPR1A microsatellite repeats and a specific single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) in the oxytocin receptor gene (OXTR) will be significantly associated with hypersexuality and uninhibited people-seeking in individuals with PEA-BP, and that this interaction will be detectable using
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study examined the transmission of AVPR1A microsatellite repeats in probands with prepubertal and early adolescent bipolar disorder phenotype (PEA-BP) and found no significant preferential transmission of AVPR1A GATA or complex repeats associated with hypersexuality or uninhibited people-seeking. The study used case-control and family-based association methods and had sufficient power to detect moderate to large effects, but still found no significant associations.

GAP: The study's null findings leave unanswered questions about the genetic underpinnings of hypersexuality and uninhibited people-seeking in PEA-BP, and whether other genetic or environmental factors contribute to these traits. Specifically, the study does not investigate potential interactions between AVPR1A microsatellite repeats and other genetic or environmental factors that may influence the development of hypersexuality and uninhibited people-seeking in PEA-BP.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the interaction between AVPR1A microsatellite repeats and a specific single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) in the oxytocin receptor gene (OXTR) will be significantly associated with hypersexuality and uninhibited people-seeking in individuals with PEA-BP, and that this interaction will be detectable using

abstract: We review our results on surgical treatment of patients with stage I non-small cell lung carcinoma and we attempted to clarify the prognostic significance of some surgical--pathologic variables. From 1993 to 1999, 667 patients received curative lung resection and complete hilar and mediastinal lymphadenectomy for non-small cell lung cancer. Of these, there were 436 Stage I disease (65%), of whom 144 T1N0 and 292 T2N0. No patients had pre- or postoperative radio- or chemotherapy. Prognostic significance of the following independent variables was tested using univariate (log-rank) and multivariate (Cox proportional-hazards) analysis: type of resection (sublobar vs lobectomy vs pneumonectomy), histology (squamous cell vs adenocarcinoma), tumour size (<or=3cm vs>3cm), histologic vascular invasion, visceral pleura involvement, positive bronchial resection margin, general T status. Overall 5-year survival was 63%. In both univariate and multivariate survival analysis, significant prognostic factors were histology (adenocarcinoma 65% vs squamous cell carcinoma 51%), tumour size (<or=3cm 67% vs>3cm 46%), and the presence of negative resection margin. Five-year survival by general T status was 66% in T1N0 vs 55% in T2N0 disease (P=0.19).

raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract include the identification of significant prognostic factors for 5-year survival in patients with stage I non-small cell lung carcinoma, which are histology (adenocarcinoma vs squamous cell carcinoma), tumor size (< or = 3cm vs > 3cm), and the presence of negative resection margin. The overall 5-year survival rate was 63%, with specific survival rates varying by these factors. For instance, adenocarcinoma patients had a 65% 5-year survival rate compared to 51% for squamous cell carcinoma patients, and tumors less than or equal to 3cm had a 67% 5-year survival rate compared to 46% for tumors greater than 3cm.

GAP: Despite identifying these prognostic factors, the study leaves unanswered the question of how these factors interact with each other and with other potential variables, such as the type of surgical resection (sublobar vs lobectomy vs pneumonectomy), to influence patient outcomes. Additionally, the study does not explore the molecular or genetic underpinnings of these prognostic factors, which could provide further insight into the disease's progression and treatment.

abstract: More than 1000 candidates applied for orthopaedic residency positions in 2014, and the competition is intense; approximately one-third of the candidates failed to secure a position in the match. However, the criteria used in the selection process often are subjective and studies have differed in terms of which criteria predict either objective measures or subjective ratings of resident performance by faculty.QUESTIONS/ Do preresidency selection factors serve as predictors of success in residency? Specifically, we asked which preresidency selection factors are associated or correlated with (1) objective measures of resident knowledge and performance; and (2) subjective ratings by faculty. Charts of 60 orthopaedic residents from our institution were reviewed. Preresidency selection criteria examined included United States Medical Licensing Examination (USMLE) Step 1 and Step 2 scores, Medical College Admission Test (MCAT) scores, number of clinical clerkship honors, number of letters of recommendation, number of away rotations, Alpha Omega Alpha (AOA) honor medical society membership, fourth-year subinternship at our institution, and number of publications. Resident performance was assessed using objective measures including American Board of Orthopaedic Surgery (ABOS) Part I scores and Orthopaedics In-Training Exam (OITE) scores and subjective ratings by faculty including global evaluation scores and faculty rankings of residents. We tested associations between preresidency criteria and the subsequent objective and subjective metrics using linear correlation analysis and Mann-Whitney tests when appropriate. Objective measures of resident performance namely, ABOS Part I scores, had a moderate linear correlation with the USMLE Step 2 scores (r = 0.55, p<0.001) and number of clinical honors received in medical school (r = 0.45, p<0.001). OITE scores had a weak linear correlation with the number of clinical honors (r = 0.35, p = 0.009) and USMLE Step 2 scores (r = 0.29, p = 0.02). With regards to subjective outcomes, AOA membership was associated with higher scores on the global evaluation (p = 0.005). AOA membership also correlated with higher global evaluation scores (r = 0.60, p = 0.005) with the strongest correlation existing between AOA membership and the "interpersonal and communication skills" subsection of the global evaluations.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that residents who are AOA members and have higher USMLE Step 2 scores will have a higher likelihood of passing the American Board of Orthopaedic Surgery (ABOS) Part II certification exam on their first attempt, compared to their non-AOA counterparts with lower USMLE Step 2 scores.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that certain preresidency selection factors, such as USMLE Step 2 scores, number of clinical honors, and AOA membership, are correlated with objective and subjective measures of resident performance, including ABOS Part I scores, OITE scores, and global evaluation scores. Specifically, USMLE Step 2 scores and number of clinical honors were moderately correlated with ABOS Part I scores, while AOA membership was strongly correlated with global evaluation scores, particularly in the "interpersonal and communication skills" subsection.

GAP: While the study identified correlations between certain preresidency selection factors and resident performance, it does not examine how these factors influence resident performance over time, particularly in terms of longitudinal outcomes such as board certification rates, career advancement, or patient satisfaction. Additionally, the study does not investigate the potential causal relationships between these factors and resident performance.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that residents who are AOA members and have higher USMLE Step 2 scores will have a higher likelihood of passing the American Board of Orthopaedic Surgery (ABOS) Part II certification exam on their first attempt, compared to their non-AOA counterparts with lower USMLE Step 2 scores.

abstract: Studies examining predictors of survival among the oldest-old have primarily focused on objective measures, such as physical function and health status. Only a few studies have examined the effect of personality traits on survival, such as optimism. The aim of this study was to examine whether an optimistic outlook predicts survival among the oldest-old. The Danish 1905 Cohort Survey is a nationwide, longitudinal survey comprising all individuals born in Denmark in 1905. At baseline in 1998, a total of 2,262 persons aged 92 or 93 agreed to participate in the intake survey. The baseline in-person interview consisted of a comprehensive questionnaire including physical functioning and health, and a question about whether the respondent had an optimistic, neutral or pessimistic outlook on his or her own future. During the follow-up period of 12 years (1998-2010) there were 2,239 deaths (99 %) in the 1905 Cohort Survey. Univariable analyses revealed that optimistic women and men were at lower risk of death compared to their neutral counterparts [HR 0.82, 95 % CI (0.73-0.93) and 0.81, 95 % CI (0.66-0.99), respectively]. When confounding factors such as baseline physical and cognitive functioning and disease were taken into account the association between optimism and survival weakened in both sexes, but the general pattern persisted. Optimistic women were still at lower risk of death compared to neutral women [HR 0.85, 95 % CI (0.74-0.97)]. The risk of death was also decreased for optimistic men compared to their neutral counterparts, but the effect was non-significant [HR 0.91, 95 % CI (0.73-1.13)].
hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimistic individuals will exhibit lower levels of systemic inflammation, as measured by C-reactive protein levels, compared to neutral or pessimistic individuals, and that this difference will mediate the association between optimism and reduced mortality risk in the oldest-old population.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that optimistic individuals, particularly women, have a lower risk of death compared to those with a neutral outlook, even after controlling for physical and cognitive functioning and disease. The association between optimism and survival is significant for women, with an hazard ratio of 0.85, but not significant for men, with a hazard ratio of 0.91. The study suggests that optimism may have a protective effect on survival in the oldest-old population.

GAP: While the study establishes a link between optimism and survival, it does not investigate the potential mechanisms by which optimism influences survival. Specifically, it does not examine whether optimism is associated with specific health behaviors or physiological responses that could contribute to increased longevity.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that optimistic individuals will exhibit lower levels of systemic inflammation, as measured by C-reactive protein levels, compared to neutral or pessimistic individuals, and that this difference will mediate the association between optimism and reduced mortality risk in the oldest-old population.

abstract: Swedish hospital mergers seem to stem from a conviction among policy makers that bigger hospitals lead to lower average costs and improved clinical outcomes. The effects of mergers in the form of multisited hospitals have not been systematically evaluated. The purpose of this article is to contribute to this area of knowledge by exploring responses to the merger of Blekinge Hospital. The evaluation was guided by the philosophy of triangulation. A questionnaire was sent to 597 randomly selected employees, that is 24% of the health care staff. Four hundred ninety-eight employees answered the questionnaire, giving a response rate of 83%. Furthermore, interviews of different groups of stakeholders were conducted. A moderate increase of quality was assessed, which, a low proportion of the employees perceived had decisively or largely to do with the merger. The majority perceives economical incentives as the drivers of change, but, at the same time, only 10% of this group believes this target was reached completely or to a large extent.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that hospitals that prioritize and communicate clear, measurable quality improvement targets to employees during the merger process will exhibit a significant positive correlation between employee perception of merger success and actual reduction in average costs per patient, compared to hospitals that primarily focus on economic incentives without clear quality targets.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The merger of Blekinge Hospital resulted in a moderate increase in quality, but only a low proportion of employees attributed this improvement to the merger itself. The majority of employees believed that economic incentives drove the changes, yet only a small percentage thought these economic targets were fully or largely met.

GAP: The study does not investigate the specific mechanisms by which hospital mergers influence clinical outcomes and cost savings, nor does it explore how employee perceptions of merger success impact the actual achievement of economic and quality goals.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that hospitals that prioritize and communicate clear, measurable quality improvement targets to employees during the merger process will exhibit a significant positive correlation between employee perception of merger success and actual reduction in average costs per patient, compared to hospitals that primarily focus on economic incentives without clear quality targets.

abstract: Orthodontic patients show high prevalence of tooth-size discrepancy. This study investigates the possible association between arch form, clinically significant tooth-size discrepancy, and sagittal molar relationship. Pretreatment orthodontic casts of 230 Saudi patients were classified into one of three arch form types (tapered, ovoid, and square) using digitally scanned images of the mandibular arches. Bolton ratio was calculated, sagittal molar relationship was defined according to Angle classification, and correlations were analyzed using ANOVA, chi-square, and t-tests. No single arch form was significantly more common than the others. Furthermore, no association was observed between the presence of significant Bolton discrepancy and the sagittal molar relationship or arch form. Overall Bolton discrepancy is significantly more prevalent in males.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that hormonal differences between males and females, specifically the influence of testosterone on dental development, contribute to the increased prevalence of Bolton discrepancy in males, and that this effect can be quantitatively measured through a comparative analysis of tooth-size ratios in relation to serum testosterone levels in a cohort of orthodontic patients.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that there is no significant association between arch form, tooth-size discrepancy (as measured by Bolton ratio), and sagittal molar relationship in orthodontic patients. Additionally, it was observed that overall Bolton discrepancy is more prevalent in males, and no single arch form is more common than the others among the studied population.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of what specific factors contribute to the higher prevalence of Bolton discrepancy in males, as the analysis did not find a correlation with arch form or sagittal molar relationship. This gap suggests a need to explore other potential factors that could influence the gender disparity in tooth-size discrepancy.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that hormonal differences between males and females, specifically the influence of testosterone on dental development, contribute to the increased prevalence of Bolton discrepancy in males, and that this effect can be quantitatively measured through a comparative analysis of tooth-size ratios in relation to serum testosterone levels in a cohort of orthodontic patients.

abstract: To prospectively evaluate the amount of tissue removed at loop electrosurgical excision procedure (LEEP) vs. cold knife conization. Forty consecutive LEEP or cold knife conization specimens were prospectively measured and weighed by a single pathology technician. Diameter, length and weight of the specimens were compared using Student's t test. Mean diameter of cold knife cone specimens was 2.6 vs. 2.2 cm for LEEP (P = .07). Mean length of cold knife cone specimens was 1.5 vs. 1.0 cm for LEEP (P = .001). Mean weight for cold knife cone specimens was 4.4 vs. 2.0 g for LEEP (P = .001).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients undergoing cold knife conization will have a lower rate of lesion recurrence compared to those undergoing LEEP due to the significantly larger volume of tissue removed, and this difference will be statistically significant at a 12-month follow-up.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found significant differences in the dimensions and weight of tissue specimens removed by cold knife conization compared to loop electrosurgical excision procedure (LEEP), with cold knife conization resulting in larger diameter (2.6 cm vs. 2.2 cm, P = 0.07), greater length (1.5 cm vs. 1.0 cm, P = 0.001), and higher weight (4.4 g vs. 2.0 g, P = 0.001) specimens.

GAP: The study provides quantitative data on the amount of tissue removed by each method but does not investigate the clinical implications of these differences, such as how the varying amounts of tissue removal might affect patient outcomes, including recurrence rates of lesions or complications.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that patients undergoing cold knife conization will have a lower rate of lesion recurrence compared to those undergoing LEEP due to the significantly larger volume of tissue removed, and this difference will be statistically significant at a 12-month follow-up.

abstract: The hypothesis was tested that pectin content and methylation degree participate in regulation of cell wall mechanical properties and in this way may affect tissue growth and freezing resistance over the course of plant cold acclimation and de-acclimation. Experiments were carried on the leaves of two double-haploid lines of winter oil-seed rape (Brassica napus subsp. oleifera), differing in winter survival and resistance to blackleg fungus (Leptosphaeria maculans). Plant acclimation in the cold (2 degrees C) brought about retardation of leaf expansion, concomitant with development of freezing resistance. These effects were associated with the increases in leaf tensile stiffness, cell wall and pectin contents, pectin methylesterase (EC 3.1.1.11) activity and the low-methylated pectin content, independently of the genotype studied. However, the cold-induced modifications in the cell wall properties were more pronounced in the leaves of the more pathogen-resistant genotype. De-acclimation promoted leaf expansion and reversed most of the cold-induced effects, with the exception of pectin methylesterase activity.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the persistent increase in pectin methylesterase activity after de-acclimation will enhance the rate and magnitude of cell wall modifications during subsequent cold acclimation events, leading to improved freezing resistance in winter oil-seed rape plants.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract include the discovery that cold acclimation in winter oil-seed rape leads to increased leaf tensile stiffness, cell wall and pectin contents, pectin methylesterase activity, and low-methylated pectin content, which are associated with the development of freezing resistance. Additionally, these cold-induced modifications are more pronounced in the leaves of the more pathogen-resistant genotype. De-acclimation reverses most of these effects except for pectin methylesterase activity.

GAP: The abstract leaves unanswered the question of how the increased pectin methylesterase activity, which persists after de-acclimation, affects the long-term maintenance of cell wall properties and freezing resistance in plants that have undergone repeated cycles of cold acclimation and de-acclimation.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the persistent increase in pectin methylesterase activity after de-acclimation will enhance the rate and magnitude of cell wall modifications during subsequent cold acclimation events, leading to improved freezing resistance in winter oil-seed rape plants.

abstract: Interference from irrelevant negative material might be a key mechanism underlying intrusive ruminative thoughts in depression. Considering commonalities between depression and social anxiety and the presence of similar intrusive thoughts in social anxiety, the current study was designed to assess whether interference from irrelevant material in working memory is specific to depression or is also present in social anxiety disorder. To examine the effects of irrelevant emotional material on working memory performance, participants memorized two lists of words on each trial and were subsequently instructed to ignore one of the lists. Participants were then asked to indicate whether a probe word belonged to the relevant list or not. Compared to control and social anxiety groups, the depression groups (both pure and comorbid with social anxiety disorder) exhibited greater difficulties removing irrelevant emotional material from working memory (i.e., greater intrusion effects). Greater intrusion effects were also associated with increased rumination. Although we included three clinical groups (depression, social anxiety, and the comorbid groups), the results are based on a relatively small number of participants.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that a cognitive training intervention aimed at improving the ability to suppress irrelevant emotional material in working memory will lead to a significant reduction in rumination and depressive symptoms in individuals with major depressive disorder, as measured by standardized clinical assessments and self-report questionnaires after a minimum of 6 weeks of intervention.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that individuals with depression, including those with comorbid social anxiety disorder, exhibit greater difficulties in removing irrelevant emotional material from working memory compared to control and social anxiety groups, and that this difficulty is associated with increased rumination.

GAP: The current study does not investigate the causal relationship between the inability to remove irrelevant emotional material from working memory and rumination in depression, nor does it explore potential therapeutic strategies that could target this cognitive mechanism to reduce rumination and improve symptoms in individuals with depression.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that a cognitive training intervention aimed at improving the ability to suppress irrelevant emotional material in working memory will lead to a significant reduction in rumination and depressive symptoms in individuals with major depressive disorder, as measured by standardized clinical assessments and self-report questionnaires after a minimum of 6 weeks of intervention.

abstract: To investigate whether prepuncture ultrasound evaluation of vascular anatomy facilitates internal jugular vein cannulation compared with landmark-guided puncture. Prospective randomized study. Single community hospital. Adult patients undergoing general anesthesia (n = 240). The right internal jugular vein was cannulated using either anatomic landmarks or prepuncture ultrasound (3.75/7.5 MHz) guidance. In the landmark group, respiratory jugular venodilation was used as the primary landmark for locating the vein. Results of cannulation and the incidence of complications were compared. Patients were randomly assigned to the ultrasound or landmark group. Respiratory jugular venodilation was identified in 188 patients (78.3%), in whom results of cannulation did not differ between the 2 techniques with respect to the venous access rate (cannulated at the first attempt: 83.5% in the landmark v 85.7% in the ultrasound group), the success rate (cannulated within 3 attempts: 96.9% v 95.6%), and the incidence of arterial puncture (1.0% v 3.3%). In the remaining 52 respiratory jugular venodilation-unidentified patients, the access rate (30.4% v 86.2%, p<0.001) and the success rate (78.3 v 100%, p<0.05) were significantly better in the ultrasound group, and no arterial puncture was recorded in the ultrasound group, whereas the incidence was 13.0% in the landmark group. The results were similar regardless of the ultrasound frequency used.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of prepuncture ultrasound evaluation will result in a significant reduction in the incidence of long-term central venous catheter-related complications, such as catheter-related bloodstream infections or thrombosis, compared to landmark-guided puncture in adult patients undergoing general anesthesia.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings of the study are that prepuncture ultrasound evaluation of vascular anatomy significantly improves the success rate and reduces complications of internal jugular vein cannulation compared to landmark-guided puncture, particularly in patients where respiratory jugular venodilation is not identified. Specifically, the ultrasound group had a higher access rate and success rate, and a lower incidence of arterial puncture, in the absence of identifiable respiratory jugular venodilation.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of whether the benefits of ultrasound guidance for internal jugular vein cannulation extend to other patient populations, such as pediatric or obese patients, who may have different anatomical characteristics that could affect the efficacy of ultrasound guidance. Additionally, the study does not investigate the long-term outcomes or patient safety implications of using ultrasound guidance for internal jugular vein cannulation.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the use of prepuncture ultrasound evaluation will result in a significant reduction in the incidence of long-term central venous catheter-related complications, such as catheter-related bloodstream infections or thrombosis, compared to landmark-guided puncture in adult patients undergoing general anesthesia.

abstract: Obesity may be associated with lower prostate specific antigen through hemodilution. We examined the relationship between body mass index and prostate specific antigen by age in men without prostate cancer in a longitudinal aging study to determine whether prostate specific antigen must be adjusted for body mass index. The study population included 994 men (4,937 observations) without prostate cancer in the Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging. Mixed effects models were used to examine the relationship between prostate specific antigen and body mass index in kg/m(2) by age. Separate models were explored in men with prostate cancer censored at diagnosis, for percent body fat measurements, for weight changes with time and adjusting for initial prostate size in 483 men (2,523 observations) with pelvic magnetic resonance imaging measurements. In men without prostate cancer body mass index was not significantly associated with prostate specific antigen after adjusting for age (p = 0.06). A 10-point body mass index increase was associated with a prostate specific antigen difference of -0.03 ng/ml (95% CI -0.40-0.49). Results were similar when men with prostate cancer were included, when percent body fat was substituted for body mass index, and after adjusting for prostate volume. Longitudinal weight changes also had no significant association with prostate specific antigen.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ratio of plasma volume to blood volume will be significantly increased in obese men compared to non-obese men, leading to a dilutional effect on PSA concentrations that is independent of prostate size or PSA production rates.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract are that body mass index (BMI) is not significantly associated with prostate specific antigen (PSA) levels after adjusting for age in men without prostate cancer, and that a 10-point increase in BMI is associated with a minimal decrease in PSA levels. The results are consistent across different models, including those that account for percent body fat, weight changes over time, and prostate volume.

GAP: One knowledge gap left unanswered by this study is the potential biological mechanism underlying the observed relationship (or lack thereof) between BMI and PSA levels. Specifically, the study mentions hemodilution as a possible explanation for lower PSA levels in obese individuals, but it does not investigate this mechanism directly or explore other potential factors that could influence the relationship between BMI and PSA.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the ratio of plasma volume to blood volume will be significantly increased in obese men compared to non-obese men, leading to a dilutional effect on PSA concentrations that is independent of prostate size or PSA production rates.

abstract: We sought to determine whether patients with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) had an objective change in aerobic fitness during cycle ergometry compared to a normal population. The most accurate test of aerobic fitness is measurement of maximum oxygen consumption (VO2max) with cycle ergometry. We performed a retrospective cohort analysis (247 patients with OSA) of VO2max from annual cycle ergometry tests compared to a large control group (normative data from 1.4 million US Air Force tests) in a tertiary care setting. Overall, individuals with OSA had increased VO2max when compared to the normalized US Air Force data (p<.001). Patients with an apnea-hypopnea index of greater than 20 demonstrated a decreased VO2max as compared to normalized values (p<.001). No differences in VO2max were observed after either medical or surgical therapy for OSA.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the increased VO2max observed in patients with mild-moderate OSA is associated with compensatory increases in cardiac output and ventilatory efficiency, which are compromised in severe OSA due to chronic hypoxia and inflammation, leading to decreased VO2max in this subgroup.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract are that individuals with obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) have increased maximum oxygen consumption (VO2max) compared to a normal population, but those with a severe apnea-hypopnea index (AHI > 20) have decreased VO2max. Additionally, there are no significant changes in VO2max after medical or surgical treatment for OSA.

GAP: The study leaves unanswered the question of what underlying physiological mechanisms are responsible for the observed changes in VO2max in patients with OSA, particularly the discrepancy between mild-moderate OSA and severe OSA. It also does not explore how these changes in aerobic fitness relate to long-term health outcomes in OSA patients.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the increased VO2max observed in patients with mild-moderate OSA is associated with compensatory increases in cardiac output and ventilatory efficiency, which are compromised in severe OSA due to chronic hypoxia and inflammation, leading to decreased VO2max in this subgroup.

abstract: It is now widely accepted that AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) is a critical regulator of energy homeostasis. Recently, it has been shown to regulate circadian clocks. In seasonal breeding species such as sheep, the circadian clock controls the secretion of an endogenous rhythm of melatonin and, as a consequence, is probably involved in the generation of seasonal rhythms of reproduction. Considering this, we identified the presence of the subunits of AMPK in different hypothalamic nuclei involved in the pre- and post-pineal pathways that control seasonality of reproduction in the ewe and we investigated if the intracerebroventricular (i.c.v.) injection of two activators of AMPK, metformin and AICAR, affected the circadian rhythm of melatonin in ewes that were housed in constant darkness. In parallel the secretion of insulin was monitored as a peripheral metabolic marker. We also investigated the effects of i.c.v. AICAR on the phosphorylation of AMPK and acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC), a downstream target of AMPK, in brain structures along the photoneuroendocrine pathway to the pineal gland. All the subunits of AMPK that we studied were identified in all brain areas that were dissected but with some differences in their level of expression among structures. Metformin and AICAR both reduced (p<0.001 and p<0.01 respectively) the amplitude of the circadian rhythm of melatonin secretion independently of insulin secretion. The i.c.v. injection of AICAR only tended (p = 0.1) to increase the levels of phosphorylated AMPK in the paraventricular nucleus but significantly increased the levels of phosphorylated ACC in the paraventricular nucleus (p<0.001) and in the pineal gland (p<0.05).
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the activation of AMPK in the suprachiasmatic nucleus will modulate the expression of clock genes in a light-dependent manner, thereby influencing the seasonal reproductive cycle in ewes, as measured by changes in the timing of the luteinizing hormone surge.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The key findings from the abstract include the presence of AMPK subunits in hypothalamic nuclei involved in regulating seasonality of reproduction in ewes, and the effect of AMPK activators (metformin and AICAR) on reducing the amplitude of the circadian rhythm of melatonin secretion without affecting insulin secretion. Additionally, AICAR increases the phosphorylation of ACC in the paraventricular nucleus and the pineal gland, indicating activation of AMPK signaling in these regions.

GAP: The current study demonstrates the impact of AMPK activation on melatonin secretion but does not elucidate the specific downstream mechanisms by which AMPK influences the circadian clock and seasonal reproduction in ewes. Furthermore, it does not investigate how these effects might be influenced by factors such as light exposure or the time of year, which are critical for seasonal breeding species.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the activation of AMPK in the suprachiasmatic nucleus will modulate the expression of clock genes in a light-dependent manner, thereby influencing the seasonal reproductive cycle in ewes, as measured by changes in the timing of the luteinizing hormone surge.

abstract: Cerebral hemispherectomy, a surgical procedure undergone to control intractable seizures, is becoming a standard procedure with more cases identified and treated early in life [33]. While the effect of the dominant hemisphere resection on spoken language has been extensively researched, little is known about reading abilities in individuals after left-sided resection. Left-lateralized phonological abilities are the key components of reading, i.e., grapheme-phoneme conversion skills [1]. These skills are critical for the acquisition of word-specific orthographic knowledge and have been shown to predict reading levels in average readers as well as in readers with mild cognitive disability [26]. Furthermore, impaired phonological processing has been implicated as the cognitive basis in struggling readers. Here, we explored the reading skills in participants who have undergone left cerebral hemispherectomy. Seven individuals who have undergone left cerebral hemispherectomy to control intractable seizures associated with perinatal infarct have been recruited for this study. We examined if components of phonological processing that are shown to reliably separate average readers from struggling readers, i.e., phonological awareness, verbal memory, speed of retrieval, and size of vocabulary, show the same relationship to reading levels when they are mediated by the right hemisphere [2]. We found that about 60% of our group developed both word reading and paragraph reading in the average range. Phonological processing measured by both phonological awareness and nonword reading was unexpectedly spared in the majority of participants. Phonological awareness levels strongly correlated with word reading. Verbal memory, a component of phonological processing skills, together with receptive vocabulary size, positively correlated with reading levels similar to those reported in average readers. Receptive vocabulary, a bilateral function, was preserved to a certain degree similar to that of strongly left-lateralized phonological skills [3]. Later seizure onset was associated with better reading levels.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degree of neural reorganization in the right hemisphere, as measured by functional MRI, will positively correlate with the level of preservation of phonological awareness and reading skills in individuals who have undergone left cerebral hemispherectomy, particularly in those with later seizure onset and shorter durations of pre-surgical seizure activity.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The study found that approximately 60% of individuals who underwent left cerebral hemispherectomy developed average-range word and paragraph reading skills, with phonological processing, including phonological awareness and nonword reading, being spared in the majority of participants. Additionally, phonological awareness levels strongly correlated with word reading, and verbal memory along with receptive vocabulary size positively correlated with reading levels. Later seizure onset was also associated with better reading levels.

GAP: While the study provides insights into reading abilities and phonological processing in individuals after left cerebral hemispherectomy, it does not investigate the specific neural mechanisms or the role of compensatory brain regions in the right hemisphere that allow for the preservation of phonological skills in these individuals. The abstract also does not explore how the age at the time of surgery or the duration of seizure activity before surgery might influence the development of reading skills post-surgery.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the degree of neural reorganization in the right hemisphere, as measured by functional MRI, will positively correlate with the level of preservation of phonological awareness and reading skills in individuals who have undergone left cerebral hemispherectomy, particularly in those with later seizure onset and shorter durations of pre-surgical seizure activity.

abstract: To characterize the gender dimorphism after injury with specific reference to the reproductive age of the women (young,<48 yrs of age, vs. old,>52 yrs of age) in a cohort of severely injured trauma patients for which significant variation in postinjury care is minimized. Secondary data analysis of an ongoing prospective multicenter cohort study. Academic, level I trauma and intensive care unit centers. Blunt-injured adults with hemorrhagic shock. None. Separate Cox proportional hazard regression models were formulated based on all patients to evaluate the effects of gender on mortality, multiple organ failure, and nosocomial infection, after controlling for all important confounders. These models were then used to characterize the effect of gender in young and old age groups. Overall mortality, multiple organ failure, and nosocomial infection rates for the entire cohort (n = 1,036) were 20%, 40%, and 45%, respectively. Mean Injury Severity Score was 32 +/- 14 (mean +/- SD). Men (n = 680) and women (n = 356) were clinically similar except that men required higher crystalloid volumes, more often had a history of alcoholism and liver disease, and had greater ventilatory and intensive care unit requirements. Female gender was independently associated with a 43% and 23% lower risk of multiple organ failure and nosocomial infection, respectively. Gender remained an independent risk factor in young and old subgroup analysis, with the protection afforded by female gender remaining unchanged.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed protective effect of female gender on the risk of multiple organ failure and nosocomial infection after severe injury is mediated by differences in sex hormone levels, specifically that higher levels of estrogen in females will correlate with reduced systemic inflammatory responses and improved outcomes in trauma patients.
raw_output:
FINDINGS: The main findings from the abstract include that female gender is independently associated with a lower risk of multiple organ failure (43% lower risk) and nosocomial infection (23% lower risk) in severely injured trauma patients. Additionally, this protective effect of female gender remains consistent across different age groups of women (young, <48 years of age, vs. old, >52 years of age). The study also notes that men and women were clinically similar except for some differences in treatment requirements and comorbidities.

GAP: One of the knowledge gaps left unanswered by this work is the underlying biological or physiological mechanisms that contribute to the observed protective effect of female gender on outcomes after severe injury. The study identifies an association but does not explore why female gender confers a lower risk of multiple organ failure and nosocomial infection.

HYPOTHESIS: We hypothesize that the observed protective effect of female gender on the risk of multiple organ failure and nosocomial infection after severe injury is mediated by differences in sex hormone levels, specifically that higher levels of estrogen in females will correlate with reduced systemic inflammatory responses and improved outcomes in trauma patients.

abstract: Communication with terminally ill patients is a main responsibility of physicians. However, many physicians feel insufficiently prepared for this task. Models of courses resulting in improvements of communicative skills of participants have been published mainly in the Anglo-American literature. This study describes the realization of a 2-day course model based on the experiences of the first three courses of this kind in Rhineland-Palatinate, and analyzes changes of participants' communication behavior. After each seminary, an evaluation form concerning participants' satisfaction with the course was filled in. Furthermore, all course participants received a questionnaire at the beginning and at the end of the course, as well as 3 months afterwards.
